content.text:
Now let me work on the Introduction section, paragraph by paragraph:

## Introduction - Paragraph 1

**Original:**
```
The human ability to hold things "in mind" is limited to a few items (Cowan, 2001), and thus when we must process and attempt to retain a stream of information, the contents of short-term memory are continually being updated. For example, as we turn the pages of a magazine, each new page becomes the present focus of attention as previous pages fall from view. Of course, we are not completely "stuck in the present", as our minds retain traces of the past, which often can be accessed and revisited when required. Our ability to access the past elements in a stream of experience (i.e. the magazine pages), is affected by the amount of time that has elapsed and the number of intervening pages between the present and the to-be-remembered past. Through the passage of time and accumulation of incoming experiences, stored memories may decay (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; Cowan, Saults, & Nugent, 1997; Hardt, Nader, & Nadel, 2013; Richards & Frankland, 2017) or become corrupted by interference (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; García-Rueda, Poch, & Campo, 2022; Wixted, 2004; Yeung, Ryan, Cowell, & Barense, 2013), causing the memory to become fuzzier and less precise.
```

**Improved:**
```
Human working memory has a severely limited capacity, typically holding only a few items at once (Cowan, 2001). Consequently, when processing continuous streams of information, working memory contents undergo constant updating. Consider reading a magazine: each new page commands immediate attention while previous pages recede from awareness. Yet we are not entirely anchored to the present moment—memory traces persist and can be retrieved when needed. However, our ability to access these past elements depends critically on both elapsed time and the number of intervening items. As time passes and new experiences accumulate, memories undergo two primary forms of degradation: temporal decay (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; Cowan, Saults, & Nugent, 1997; Hardt, Nader, & Nadel, 2013; Richards & Frankland, 2017) and interference-based corruption (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; García-Rueda, Poch, & Campo, 2022; Wixted, 2004; Yeung, Ryan, Cowell, & Barense, 2013). Both processes progressively diminish memory precision and fidelity.
```

Both processes progressively diminish memory precision and fidelity.

+## 1. Introduction

+Human working memory has a severely limited capacity, typically holding only a few items at once (Cowan, 2001). Consequently, when processing continuous streams of information, working memory contents undergo constant updating. Consider reading a magazine: each new page commands immediate attention while previous pages recede from awareness. Yet we are not entirely anchored to the present moment—memory traces persist and can be retrieved when needed. However, our ability to access these past elements depends critically on both elapsed time and the number of intervening items. Both processes progressively diminish memory precision and fidelity.

content.text:
## Introduction - Paragraph 2

**Original:**
```
The time-course of memory decay for a continuous stream of information has been well-established in the context of recognition memory for words, where the goal is simply to detect whether a currently presented item has been presented previously in the task. Indeed, through the use of the continuous recognition paradigm (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961), which requires participants to indicate whether each item in a sequential stream of stimuli is "old" or "new", researchers have found that memory accuracy declines in a roughly exponential fashion with respect to time or the number of intervening items (Buchsbaum, Padmanabhan, & Berman, 2011; Hintzman, 1969; Hockley, 1982; Rubin & Wenzel, 1996). However, while the standard continuous recognition procedure can be used to estimate how memory strength declines with time and interference, it is not an ideal procedure for examining the time course of memory precision (Brady, Robinson, & Williams, 2024). Because the similarity between target and novel items is often low, the task can be completed by relying on a coarser global match signal to accurately reject a foil, instead of accessing the precise low level details of the memory.
```

**Improved:**
```
The temporal dynamics of memory decay during continuous information processing have been extensively characterized using word recognition tasks. In the classic continuous recognition paradigm (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961), participants classify each item in a sequential stream as either "old" (previously seen) or "new" (novel). This paradigm has consistently revealed that recognition accuracy follows an approximately exponential decay function, declining predictably with both elapsed time and intervening items (Buchsbaum, Padmanabhan, & Berman, 2011; Hintzman, 1969; Hockley, 1982; Rubin & Wenzel, 1996). However, standard continuous recognition procedures have a critical limitation: they poorly assess memory precision (Brady, Robinson, & Williams, 2024). The typically low similarity between targets and foils allows participants to succeed using coarse-grained global matching strategies rather than retrieving fine-grained mnemonic details. This methodological constraint has left the temporal evolution of memory precision largely unexplored.
```

Human working memory has a severely limited capacity, typically holding only a few items at once (Cowan, 2001). Consequently, when processing continuous streams of information, working memory contents undergo constant updating. Consider reading a magazine: each new page commands immediate attention while previous pages recede from awareness. Yet we are not entirely anchored to the present moment—memory traces persist and can be retrieved when needed. The temporal dynamics of memory decay during continuous information processing have been extensively characterized using word recognition tasks. In the classic continuous recognition paradigm (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961), participants classify each item in a sequential stream as either "old" (previously seen) or "new" (novel). The typically low similarity between targets and foils allows participants to succeed using coarse-grained global matching strategies rather than retrieving fine-grained mnemonic details. This methodological constraint has left the temporal evolution of memory precision largely unexplored.

## 1. Introduction

Human working memory has a severely limited capacity, typically holding only a few items at once (Cowan, 2001). Consequently, when processing continuous streams of information, working memory contents undergo constant updating. Consider reading a magazine: each new page commands immediate attention while previous pages recede from awareness. Yet we are not entirely anchored to the present moment—memory traces persist and can be retrieved when needed. However, our ability to access these past elements depends critically on both elapsed time and the number of intervening items. As time passes and new experiences accumulate, memories undergo two primary forms of degradation: temporal decay (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; Cowan, Saults, & Nugent, 1997; Hardt, Nader, & Nadel, 2013; Richards & Frankland, 2017) and interference-based corruption (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; García-Rueda, Poch, & Campo, 2022; Wixted, 2004; Yeung, Ryan, Cowell, & Barense, 2013). Both processes progressively diminish memory precision and fidelity.

content.text:
I'll continue with the remaining Introduction paragraphs. Let me continue with paragraph 3:

## Introduction - Paragraph 3

**Original:**
```
A well-known recognition paradigm that assesses mnemonic precision for visual stimuli is the mnemonic similarity task (MST) (Kirwan, Jones, Miller, & Stark, 2007; Stark, Kirwan, & Stark, 2019), which is designed to place demand on "pattern separation"—an hypothesized neural process that encodes orthogonal mnemonic representations from overlapping inputs. Typically employed in a study-test format, participants are initially presented with a series of target objects, often in an incidental encoding task. In a following test phase, participants perform a difficult recognition task. Thus, in the test phase, target objects are intermixed with novel items and 'similar lures' which are perceptually similar to targets. The difficulty of the MST can be manipulated by parametrically manipulating the similarity between targets and lures (Lacy, Yassa, Stark, Muftuler, & Stark, 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012), and indeed, it has been found that false alarm rates increases with target-lure similarity (Kim & Yassa, 2013; Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012; Stark et al., 2019; Stark, Stevenson, Wu, Rutledge, & Stark, 2015). Over the years, several variations of the MST have been developed, including a continuous recognition variant (Lacy et al., 2011; Stark et al., 2015; Yassa et al., 2010). With the continuous variant of the MST, the decay of mnemonic precision can be investigated by manipulating the number of intervening items between the initial presentation of a memory target and a second presentation where it is either repeated or replaced with a similar lure. Unsurprisingly, hit and correct rejection rates have been found to decline as the number of intervening items between presentations increases (Ally, Hussey, Ko, & Molitor, 2013; Molitor, Ko, Hussey, & Ally, 2014; Stark et al., 2015). However, these studies examined longer time frames, probing memory after 4, 12, or 40 intervening items (Ally et al., 2013; Molitor et al., 2014) or randomly from a range of 0-9, 20-80, or 120-180 intervening items (Stark et al., 2015), and it remains an open question of how mnemonic precision may change over short durations such as after only one intervening item.
```

**Improved:**
```
The mnemonic similarity task (MST) provides a powerful paradigm for assessing memory precision with visual stimuli (Kirwan, Jones, Miller, & Stark, 2007; Stark, Kirwan, & Stark, 2019). This task specifically targets "pattern separation"—the neural mechanism that creates distinct memory representations from overlapping perceptual inputs. In its standard study-test format, participants first encode a series of target objects, often through incidental learning. During the subsequent test phase, they must discriminate between three stimulus types: repeated targets, novel foils, and critically, "similar lures" that share perceptual features with the targets. Task difficulty can be systematically manipulated by varying target-lure similarity (Lacy, Yassa, Stark, Muftuler, & Stark, 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). Research consistently demonstrates that false alarm rates increase monotonically with target-lure similarity (Kim & Yassa, 2013; Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012; Stark et al., 2019; Stark, Stevenson, Wu, Rutledge, & Stark, 2015).

Critically, researchers have developed a continuous recognition variant of the MST (Lacy et al., 2011; Stark et al., 2015; Yassa et al., 2010) that enables investigation of memory precision decay over time. By manipulating the lag between initial target presentation and subsequent test (either as repetition or similar lure), this paradigm tracks how precision deteriorates with intervening items. Previous studies have documented declining hit rates and correct rejections with increasing lag (Ally, Hussey, Ko, & Molitor, 2013; Molitor, Ko, Hussey, & Ally, 2014; Stark et al., 2015). However, these investigations have focused exclusively on longer intervals—testing after 4, 12, or 40 intervening items (Ally et al., 2013; Molitor et al., 2014) or sampling from ranges of 0-9, 20-80, or 120-180 items (Stark et al., 2015). The critical question of how mnemonic precision changes at very short intervals, particularly after just a single intervening item, remains unexplored.
```

In the classic continuous recognition paradigm (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961), participants classify each item in a sequential stream as either "old" (previously seen) or "new" (novel). This paradigm has consistently revealed that recognition accuracy follows an approximately exponential decay function, declining predictably with both elapsed time and intervening items (Buchsbaum, Padmanabhan, & Berman, 2011; Hintzman, 1969; Hockley, 1982; Rubin & Wenzel, 1996). However, standard continuous recognition procedures have a critical limitation: they poorly assess memory precision (Brady, Robinson, & Williams, 2024). The typically low similarity between targets and foils allows participants to succeed using coarse-grained global matching strategies rather than retrieving fine-grained mnemonic details. This methodological constraint has left the temporal evolution of memory precision largely unexplored.

You make an excellent point. Let me revise that paragraph to better position the existing literature and highlight the gap our study addresses:

**Revised Paragraph 3:**

```
The mnemonic similarity task (MST) provides a powerful paradigm for assessing memory precision with visual stimuli (Kirwan, Jones, Miller, & Stark, 2007; Stark, Kirwan, & Stark, 2019). This task specifically targets "pattern separation"—the neural mechanism that creates distinct memory representations from overlapping perceptual inputs. In its standard study-test format, participants first encode a series of target objects, often through incidental learning. During the subsequent test phase, they must discriminate between three stimulus types: repeated targets, novel foils, and critically, "similar lures" that share perceptual features with the targets. Task difficulty can be systematically manipulated by varying target-lure similarity (Lacy, Yassa, Stark, Muftuler, & Stark, 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). Research consistently demonstrates that false alarm rates increase monotonically with target-lure similarity (Kim & Yassa, 2013; Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012; Stark et al., 2019; Stark, Stevenson, Wu, Rutledge, & Stark, 2015).

Although researchers have developed a continuous recognition variant of the MST (Lacy et al., 2011; Stark et al., 2015; Yassa et al., 2010), existing implementations have notable limitations. By manipulating the lag between initial target presentation and subsequent test (either as repetition or similar lure), this paradigm can track precision changes over time. Previous studies have documented declining hit rates and correct rejections with increasing lag (Ally, Hussey, Ko, & Molitor, 2013; Molitor, Ko, Hussey, & Ally, 2014; Stark et al., 2015). However, these investigations have examined only extended intervals—testing after 4, 12, or 40 intervening items (Ally et al., 2013; Molitor et al., 2014) or sampling from ranges of 0-9, 20-80, or 120-180 items (Stark et al., 2015). This focus on longer delays has left a crucial gap: we do not know how mnemonic precision changes at very short intervals, particularly after just a single intervening item, where working memory processes should still be active.
```

toolUseResult.originalFile:
Rapid Decay of Mnemonic Precision: Evidence from Short-Interval Continuous Recognition

Corey Loo¹,², Bradley Buchsbaum¹,²

¹Rotman Research Institute, Baycrest Health Sciences
Toronto, Ontario M6A 2E1, Canada

²Department of Psychology, University of Toronto
Toronto, Ontario M5S 1A1, Canada

## Abstract

[Note: Abstract is missing in the original manuscript and needs to be written]

## 1. Introduction

Human working memory has a severely limited capacity, typically holding only a few items at once (Cowan, 2001). Consequently, when processing continuous streams of information, working memory contents undergo constant updating. Consider reading a magazine: each new page commands immediate attention while previous pages recede from awareness. Yet we are not entirely anchored to the present moment—memory traces persist and can be retrieved when needed. However, our ability to access these past elements depends critically on both elapsed time and the number of intervening items. As time passes and new experiences accumulate, memories undergo two primary forms of degradation: temporal decay (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; Cowan, Saults, & Nugent, 1997; Hardt, Nader, & Nadel, 2013; Richards & Frankland, 2017) and interference-based corruption (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; García-Rueda, Poch, & Campo, 2022; Wixted, 2004; Yeung, Ryan, Cowell, & Barense, 2013). Both processes progressively diminish memory precision and fidelity.

The temporal dynamics of memory decay during continuous information processing have been extensively characterized using word recognition tasks. In the classic continuous recognition paradigm (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961), participants classify each item in a sequential stream as either "old" (previously seen) or "new" (novel). This paradigm has consistently revealed that recognition accuracy follows an approximately exponential decay function, declining predictably with both elapsed time and intervening items (Buchsbaum, Padmanabhan, & Berman, 2011; Hintzman, 1969; Hockley, 1982; Rubin & Wenzel, 1996). However, standard continuous recognition procedures have a critical limitation: they poorly assess memory precision (Brady, Robinson, & Williams, 2024). The typically low similarity between targets and foils allows participants to succeed using coarse-grained global matching strategies rather than retrieving fine-grained mnemonic details. This methodological constraint has left the temporal evolution of memory precision largely unexplored.

content.text:
Now let me continue with paragraph 4 of the Introduction:

## Introduction - Paragraph 4

**Original:**
```
Similar investigations on the time course for mnemonic precision have been conducted in studies utilizing verbal stimuli. A pair of studies by Jones and Acthley (2002; 2006) have examined how memory decays over short periods of time when more precise verbal memories are required due to the embedding of similar items as foils. This is achieved through the use of a feature-conjunction paradigm, where participants are presented with a sequence of compound words in a continuous recognition task. Throughout the task, some of the compound words are repeated, with the number of intervening items between the initial and subsequent presentation being manipulated (0, 1, 5, 10, 20 intervening items). On occasion, the repeated word is replaced with a conjunction lure word, a word formed from the rearrangement of morphemes from two previously studied words (e.g., the prefix and suffix from the studied words blackmail and jailbird are recombined to form the conjunction lure blackbird). Interestingly, false alarm rates to these conjunction lures followed a curvilinear trend, initially increasing sharply between lag 0 and lag 1 and then followed by a gradual decrease in false alarms as the number of intervening items increased. Jones and Acthley theorized that this curvilinear pattern relates to the relative decay of different memory signals, incorporating dual-process theories of recognition memory (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Jacoby, 1991; Jones, Jacoby, & Gellis, 2001; Mandler, 1980, 1991; Yonelinas, 1994, 1997, 2002) to account for their findings.
```

**Improved:**
```
Parallel investigations using verbal stimuli have revealed intriguing patterns of short-term mnemonic precision decay. Jones and Atchley (2002; 2006) examined this phenomenon using a feature-conjunction paradigm that demands precise verbal memory discrimination. In their continuous recognition task, participants encountered sequences of compound words, some of which were repeated after varying lags (0, 1, 5, 10, or 20 intervening items). Crucially, some repetitions were replaced with conjunction lures—novel compounds created by recombining morphemes from previously studied words (e.g., "blackmail" and "jailbird" recombined to form the lure "blackbird"). These conjunction lures revealed a striking curvilinear pattern of false alarms: an initial sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1, followed by a gradual decline at longer lags. Jones and Atchley interpreted this pattern through dual-process recognition theory (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Jacoby, 1991; Jones, Jacoby, & Gellis, 2001; Mandler, 1980, 1991; Yonelinas, 1994, 1997, 2002), suggesting that the relative decay rates of distinct memory signals produce this characteristic temporal profile.
```

However, these investigations have examined only extended intervals—testing after 4, 12, or 40 intervening items (Ally et al., 2013; Molitor et al., 2014) or sampling from ranges of 0-9, 20-80, or 120-180 items (Stark et al., 2015). Rapid Decay of Mnemonic Precision: Evidence from Short-Interval Continuous Recognition

Corey Loo¹,², Bradley Buchsbaum¹,²

¹Rotman Research Institute, Baycrest Health Sciences
Toronto, Ontario M6A 2E1, Canada

²Department of Psychology, University of Toronto
Toronto, Ontario M5S 1A1, Canada

## Abstract

[Note: Abstract is missing in the original manuscript and needs to be written]

## 1. Introduction

Human working memory has a severely limited capacity, typically holding only a few items at once (Cowan, 2001). Consequently, when processing continuous streams of information, working memory contents undergo constant updating. Consider reading a magazine: each new page commands immediate attention while previous pages recede from awareness. Yet we are not entirely anchored to the present moment—memory traces persist and can be retrieved when needed. However, our ability to access these past elements depends critically on both elapsed time and the number of intervening items. As time passes and new experiences accumulate, memories undergo two primary forms of degradation: temporal decay (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; Cowan, Saults, & Nugent, 1997; Hardt, Nader, & Nadel, 2013; Richards & Frankland, 2017) and interference-based corruption (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; García-Rueda, Poch, & Campo, 2022; Wixted, 2004; Yeung, Ryan, Cowell, & Barense, 2013). Both processes progressively diminish memory precision and fidelity.

The temporal dynamics of memory decay during continuous information processing have been extensively characterized using word recognition tasks. In the classic continuous recognition paradigm (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961), participants classify each item in a sequential stream as either "old" (previously seen) or "new" (novel). This paradigm has consistently revealed that recognition accuracy follows an approximately exponential decay function, declining predictably with both elapsed time and intervening items (Buchsbaum, Padmanabhan, & Berman, 2011; Hintzman, 1969; Hockley, 1982; Rubin & Wenzel, 1996). However, standard continuous recognition procedures have a critical limitation: they poorly assess memory precision (Brady, Robinson, & Williams, 2024). The typically low similarity between targets and foils allows participants to succeed using coarse-grained global matching strategies rather than retrieving fine-grained mnemonic details. This methodological constraint has left the temporal evolution of memory precision largely unexplored.

The mnemonic similarity task (MST) provides a powerful paradigm for assessing memory precision with visual stimuli (Kirwan, Jones, Miller, & Stark, 2007; Stark, Kirwan, & Stark, 2019). This task specifically targets "pattern separation"—the neural mechanism that creates distinct memory representations from overlapping perceptual inputs. In its standard study-test format, participants first encode a series of target objects, often through incidental learning. During the subsequent test phase, they must discriminate between three stimulus types: repeated targets, novel foils, and critically, "similar lures" that share perceptual features with the targets. Task difficulty can be systematically manipulated by varying target-lure similarity (Lacy, Yassa, Stark, Muftuler, & Stark, 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). Research consistently demonstrates that false alarm rates increase monotonically with target-lure similarity (Kim & Yassa, 2013; Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012; Stark et al., 2019; Stark, Stevenson, Wu, Rutledge, & Stark, 2015).

Although researchers have developed a continuous recognition variant of the MST (Lacy et al., 2011; Stark et al., 2015; Yassa et al., 2010), existing implementations have notable limitations. By manipulating the lag between initial target presentation and subsequent test (either as repetition or similar lure), this paradigm can track precision changes over time. Previous studies have documented declining hit rates and correct rejections with increasing lag (Ally, Hussey, Ko, & Molitor, 2013; Molitor, Ko, Hussey, & Ally, 2014; Stark et al., 2015). However, these investigations have examined only extended intervals—testing after 4, 12, or 40 intervening items (Ally et al., 2013; Molitor et al., 2014) or sampling from ranges of 0-9, 20-80, or 120-180 items (Stark et al., 2015). This focus on longer delays has left a crucial gap: we do not know how mnemonic precision changes at very short intervals, particularly after just a single intervening item, where working memory processes should still be active.

content.text:
Let me continue with paragraph 5:

## Introduction - Paragraph 5

**Original:**
```
Dual-process theory propose that recognition can occur through two separate processes: recollection and familiarity (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Diana, Yonelinas, & Ranganath, 2010; Yonelinas, 2002). During recollection based recognition, memory for the precise contextual details of the episodic event are recalled. Familiarity based recognition lacks this recall of context specific details and instead is driven by a global match signal between the percept and an item stored in memory. This produces a general feeling that the item or event has been encountered before. Similar lures, having a high degree of overlapping features with items in memory, elicit strong familiarity signals, often leading to their false endorsement as being previously seen. This familiarity can be overcome with recollect-to-reject strategies, where the recall of the precise features of the original percept is used to compare against the features of the lure. However, memory for these high precision, episode-specific details associated with recollection are thought to decay rapidly in short term memory (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006, 2008; Matzen, Taylor, & Benjamin, 2011), while the global match signals related to familiarity persist for much longer. Thus, when recognition is immediate, the precise details remain accessible and can be used to reject the lure. However, after a short period of time, high precision information for these features tends to decay, while familiarity remains relatively strong.
```

**Improved:**
```
Dual-process theory posits that recognition memory operates through two distinct mechanisms: recollection and familiarity (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Diana, Yonelinas, & Ranganath, 2010; Yonelinas, 2002). Recollection involves retrieving specific contextual details from the original encoding episode—a precise, high-fidelity process. In contrast, familiarity relies on a global match signal between current perception and stored memory traces, generating a diffuse sense of prior encounter without specific episodic detail. This distinction becomes critical when confronting similar lures: their overlapping features with stored targets trigger strong familiarity signals, increasing false recognition risk. Participants can employ "recollect-to-reject" strategies, using retrieved episodic details to differentiate lures from genuine targets. However, the temporal dynamics strongly favor familiarity. High-precision episodic details decay rapidly from short-term memory (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006, 2008; Matzen, Taylor, & Benjamin, 2011), while familiarity signals persist considerably longer. Consequently, immediate recognition tests allow successful lure rejection through accessible episodic details, but even brief delays shift the balance toward familiarity-based errors as precision information becomes unavailable.
```

Parallel investigations using verbal stimuli have revealed intriguing patterns of short-term mnemonic precision decay. Jones and Atchley (2002; 2006) examined this phenomenon using a feature-conjunction paradigm that demands precise verbal memory discrimination. In their continuous recognition task, participants encountered sequences of compound words, some of which were repeated after varying lags (0, 1, 5, 10, or 20 intervening items). Crucially, some repetitions were replaced with conjunction lures—novel compounds created by recombining morphemes from previously studied words (e.g., "blackmail" and "jailbird" recombined to form the lure "blackbird"). These conjunction lures revealed a striking curvilinear pattern of false alarms: an initial sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1, followed by a gradual decline at longer lags. Jones and Atchley interpreted this pattern through dual-process recognition theory (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Jacoby, 1991; Jones, Jacoby, & Gellis, 2001; Mandler, 1980, 1991; Yonelinas, 1994, 1997, 2002), suggesting that the relative decay rates of distinct memory signals produce this characteristic temporal profile.

Dual-process theory posits that recognition memory operates through two distinct mechanisms: recollection and familiarity (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Diana, Yonelinas, & Ranganath, 2010; Yonelinas, 2002). Recollection involves retrieving specific contextual details from the original encoding episode—a precise, high-fidelity process. In contrast, familiarity relies on a global match signal between current perception and stored memory traces, generating a diffuse sense of prior encounter without specific episodic detail. This distinction becomes critical when confronting similar lures: their overlapping features with stored targets trigger strong familiarity signals, increasing false recognition risk. Participants can employ "recollect-to-reject" strategies, using retrieved episodic details to differentiate lures from genuine targets. However, the temporal dynamics strongly favor familiarity. High-precision episodic details decay rapidly from short-term memory (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006, 2008; Matzen, Taylor, & Benjamin, 2011), while familiarity signals persist considerably longer. Consequently, immediate recognition tests allow successful lure rejection through accessible episodic details, but even brief delays shift the balance toward familiarity-based errors as precision information becomes unavailable.

Jones and Atchley interpreted this pattern through dual-process recognition theory (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Jacoby, 1991; Jones, Jacoby, & Gellis, 2001; Mandler, 1980, 1991; Yonelinas, 1994, 1997, 2002), suggesting that the relative decay rates of distinct memory signals produce this characteristic temporal profile.

Previous studies have documented declining hit rates and correct rejections with increasing lag (Ally, Hussey, Ko, & Molitor, 2013; Molitor, Ko, Hussey, & Ally, 2014; Stark et al., 2015). +Parallel investigations using verbal stimuli have revealed intriguing patterns of short-term mnemonic precision decay. Jones and Atchley (2002; 2006) examined this phenomenon using a feature-conjunction paradigm that demands precise verbal memory discrimination. In their continuous recognition task, participants encountered sequences of compound words, some of which were repeated after varying lags (0, 1, 5, 10, or 20 intervening items). Crucially, some repetitions were replaced with conjunction lures—novel compounds created by recombining morphemes from previously studied words (e.g., "blackmail" and "jailbird" recombined to form the lure "blackbird"). These conjunction lures revealed a striking curvilinear pattern of false alarms: an initial sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1, followed by a gradual decline at longer lags. Jones and Atchley interpreted this pattern through dual-process recognition theory (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Jacoby, 1991; Jones, Jacoby, & Gellis, 2001; Mandler, 1980, 1991; Yonelinas, 1994, 1997, 2002), suggesting that the relative decay rates of distinct memory signals produce this characteristic temporal profile.

+Dual-process theory posits that recognition memory operates through two distinct mechanisms: recollection and familiarity (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Diana, Yonelinas, & Ranganath, 2010; Yonelinas, 2002). Recollection involves retrieving specific contextual details from the original encoding episode—a precise, high-fidelity process. In contrast, familiarity relies on a global match signal between current perception and stored memory traces, generating a diffuse sense of prior encounter without specific episodic detail. This distinction becomes critical when confronting similar lures: their overlapping features with stored targets trigger strong familiarity signals, increasing false recognition risk. Participants can employ "recollect-to-reject" strategies, using retrieved episodic details to differentiate lures from genuine targets. However, the temporal dynamics strongly favor familiarity. High-precision episodic details decay rapidly from short-term memory (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006, 2008; Matzen, Taylor, & Benjamin, 2011), while familiarity signals persist considerably longer. Consequently, immediate recognition tests allow successful lure rejection through accessible episodic details, but even brief delays shift the balance toward familiarity-based errors as precision information becomes unavailable.
\ No newline at end of file

Let me continue with paragraph 6:

## Introduction - Paragraph 6

**Original:**
```
An additional factor to consider is the difficulty of the discrimination to be made. Studies using the MST have demonstrated that discrimination performance becomes more difficult as the similarity between targets and lures increases, which is thought to reflect difficulties in overcoming the familiarity signals evoked by lures (Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). How might varying the amount of familiarity evoked by a lure via its similarity to a memory target interact with the decay of mnemonic precision? Investigations with conjunction word lures indicate that lures are more difficult to reject as recollection signals decline, resulting in a reliance on familiarity signals (Jones & Atchley, 2006). One can imagine then, that the difficulties in overcoming the familiarity signals elicited by increasingly similar lures and the decay of mnemonic precision would have a synergistic effect.
```

**Improved:**
```
Beyond temporal factors, discrimination difficulty represents another critical dimension affecting memory precision. MST studies consistently demonstrate that performance deteriorates as target-lure similarity increases, reflecting the mounting challenge of overcoming familiarity signals from highly similar lures (Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). This raises a crucial question: how does lure similarity interact with the temporal decay of mnemonic precision? Evidence from conjunction lure studies suggests a synergistic relationship—as recollection signals weaken over time, participants increasingly rely on familiarity, making similar lures progressively harder to reject (Jones & Atchley, 2006). We hypothesize that this interaction should produce a multiplicative effect: the rapid decay of precision information combined with strong familiarity signals from similar lures should create particularly steep performance declines at short intervals.
```

Dual-process theory posits that recognition memory operates through two distinct mechanisms: recollection and familiarity (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Diana, Yonelinas, & Ranganath, 2010; Yonelinas, 2002). Recollection involves retrieving specific contextual details from the original encoding episode—a precise, high-fidelity process. In contrast, familiarity relies on a global match signal between current perception and stored memory traces, generating a diffuse sense of prior encounter without specific episodic detail. This distinction becomes critical when confronting similar lures: their overlapping features with stored targets trigger strong familiarity signals, increasing false recognition risk. High-precision episodic details decay rapidly from short-term memory (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006, 2008; Matzen, Taylor, & Benjamin, 2011), while familiarity signals persist considerably longer. Consequently, immediate recognition tests allow successful lure rejection through accessible episodic details, but even brief delays shift the balance toward familiarity-based errors as precision information becomes unavailable.

Beyond temporal factors, discrimination difficulty represents another critical dimension affecting memory precision. MST studies consistently demonstrate that performance deteriorates as target-lure similarity increases, reflecting the mounting challenge of overcoming familiarity signals from highly similar lures (Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). This raises a crucial question: how does lure similarity interact with the temporal decay of mnemonic precision? Evidence from conjunction lure studies suggests a synergistic relationship—as recollection signals weaken over time, participants increasingly rely on familiarity, making similar lures progressively harder to reject (Jones & Atchley, 2006). We hypothesize that this interaction should produce a multiplicative effect: the rapid decay of precision information combined with strong familiarity signals from similar lures should create particularly steep performance declines at short intervals.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
Rapid Decay of Mnemonic Precision: Evidence from Short-Interval Continuous Recognition

Corey Loo¹,², Bradley Buchsbaum¹,²

¹Rotman Research Institute, Baycrest Health Sciences
Toronto, Ontario M6A 2E1, Canada

²Department of Psychology, University of Toronto
Toronto, Ontario M5S 1A1, Canada

## Abstract

[Note: Abstract is missing in the original manuscript and needs to be written]

## 1. Introduction

Human working memory has a severely limited capacity, typically holding only a few items at once (Cowan, 2001). Consequently, when processing continuous streams of information, working memory contents undergo constant updating. Consider reading a magazine: each new page commands immediate attention while previous pages recede from awareness. Yet we are not entirely anchored to the present moment—memory traces persist and can be retrieved when needed. However, our ability to access these past elements depends critically on both elapsed time and the number of intervening items. As time passes and new experiences accumulate, memories undergo two primary forms of degradation: temporal decay (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; Cowan, Saults, & Nugent, 1997; Hardt, Nader, & Nadel, 2013; Richards & Frankland, 2017) and interference-based corruption (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; García-Rueda, Poch, & Campo, 2022; Wixted, 2004; Yeung, Ryan, Cowell, & Barense, 2013). Both processes progressively diminish memory precision and fidelity.

The temporal dynamics of memory decay during continuous information processing have been extensively characterized using word recognition tasks. In the classic continuous recognition paradigm (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961), participants classify each item in a sequential stream as either "old" (previously seen) or "new" (novel). This paradigm has consistently revealed that recognition accuracy follows an approximately exponential decay function, declining predictably with both elapsed time and intervening items (Buchsbaum, Padmanabhan, & Berman, 2011; Hintzman, 1969; Hockley, 1982; Rubin & Wenzel, 1996). However, standard continuous recognition procedures have a critical limitation: they poorly assess memory precision (Brady, Robinson, & Williams, 2024). The typically low similarity between targets and foils allows participants to succeed using coarse-grained global matching strategies rather than retrieving fine-grained mnemonic details. This methodological constraint has left the temporal evolution of memory precision largely unexplored.

The mnemonic similarity task (MST) provides a powerful paradigm for assessing memory precision with visual stimuli (Kirwan, Jones, Miller, & Stark, 2007; Stark, Kirwan, & Stark, 2019). This task specifically targets "pattern separation"—the neural mechanism that creates distinct memory representations from overlapping perceptual inputs. In its standard study-test format, participants first encode a series of target objects, often through incidental learning. During the subsequent test phase, they must discriminate between three stimulus types: repeated targets, novel foils, and critically, "similar lures" that share perceptual features with the targets. Task difficulty can be systematically manipulated by varying target-lure similarity (Lacy, Yassa, Stark, Muftuler, & Stark, 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). Research consistently demonstrates that false alarm rates increase monotonically with target-lure similarity (Kim & Yassa, 2013; Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012; Stark et al., 2019; Stark, Stevenson, Wu, Rutledge, & Stark, 2015).

Although researchers have developed a continuous recognition variant of the MST (Lacy et al., 2011; Stark et al., 2015; Yassa et al., 2010), existing implementations have notable limitations. By manipulating the lag between initial target presentation and subsequent test (either as repetition or similar lure), this paradigm can track precision changes over time. Previous studies have documented declining hit rates and correct rejections with increasing lag (Ally, Hussey, Ko, & Molitor, 2013; Molitor, Ko, Hussey, & Ally, 2014; Stark et al., 2015). However, these investigations have examined only extended intervals—testing after 4, 12, or 40 intervening items (Ally et al., 2013; Molitor et al., 2014) or sampling from ranges of 0-9, 20-80, or 120-180 items (Stark et al., 2015). This focus on longer delays has left a crucial gap: we do not know how mnemonic precision changes at very short intervals, particularly after just a single intervening item, where working memory processes should still be active.

Parallel investigations using verbal stimuli have revealed intriguing patterns of short-term mnemonic precision decay. Jones and Atchley (2002; 2006) examined this phenomenon using a feature-conjunction paradigm that demands precise verbal memory discrimination. In their continuous recognition task, participants encountered sequences of compound words, some of which were repeated after varying lags (0, 1, 5, 10, or 20 intervening items). Crucially, some repetitions were replaced with conjunction lures—novel compounds created by recombining morphemes from previously studied words (e.g., "blackmail" and "jailbird" recombined to form the lure "blackbird"). These conjunction lures revealed a striking curvilinear pattern of false alarms: an initial sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1, followed by a gradual decline at longer lags. Jones and Atchley interpreted this pattern through dual-process recognition theory (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Jacoby, 1991; Jones, Jacoby, & Gellis, 2001; Mandler, 1980, 1991; Yonelinas, 1994, 1997, 2002), suggesting that the relative decay rates of distinct memory signals produce this characteristic temporal profile.

Dual-process theory posits that recognition memory operates through two distinct mechanisms: recollection and familiarity (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Diana, Yonelinas, & Ranganath, 2010; Yonelinas, 2002). Recollection involves retrieving specific contextual details from the original encoding episode—a precise, high-fidelity process. In contrast, familiarity relies on a global match signal between current perception and stored memory traces, generating a diffuse sense of prior encounter without specific episodic detail. This distinction becomes critical when confronting similar lures: their overlapping features with stored targets trigger strong familiarity signals, increasing false recognition risk. Participants can employ "recollect-to-reject" strategies, using retrieved episodic details to differentiate lures from genuine targets. However, the temporal dynamics strongly favor familiarity. High-precision episodic details decay rapidly from short-term memory (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006, 2008; Matzen, Taylor, & Benjamin, 2011), while familiarity signals persist considerably longer. Consequently, immediate recognition tests allow successful lure rejection through accessible episodic details, but even brief delays shift the balance toward familiarity-based errors as precision information becomes unavailable.

content.text:
Now let me work on the final paragraph of the Introduction:

## Introduction - Paragraph 7

**Original:**
```
In the current study, we test the hypothesis that the rate of decline of lure discrimination performance increases with the similarity of lure probes. In a continuous recognition task we manipulated the number of intervening items (lag) between the first presentation of a target image and the presentation of its repetition (or a similar lure image). To aid in our analysis, we developed a measure of target-lure similarity by training a model to predict the probability that a lure will elicit a false alarm from feature similarity metrics derived from the VGG-16 network, a convolutional neural network trained to classify images (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015). We reasoned that high fidelity information details decay more quickly than familiarity based global match signals, and this will be reflected in quadratic pattern in false alarm rates: a sharp increase in false alarms as the number of intervening items increases from 0 to 1 (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006). Additionally, target-lure similarity should modulate this trend such that the apex of the quadratic trend increases with target-lure similarity. By manipulating both study-test lag and the difficulty of the recognition judgement, we can map how the fidelity of memory decreases—relative to familiarity- or gist-based memory—as new experiences accumulate in a stream of experience.
```

**Improved:**
```
The present study directly tests whether lure similarity modulates the temporal decay of mnemonic precision at short intervals. Using a continuous recognition paradigm, we systematically manipulated lag (0-32 intervening items) between initial target presentation and subsequent test with either repetition or similar lure. To quantify target-lure similarity objectively, we developed a novel approach: training a predictive model using feature representations from VGG-16, a deep convolutional neural network (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015), to estimate false alarm probability for each lure based on its perceptual similarity to the target. 

Our central hypothesis predicts a characteristic temporal signature of precision decay. Based on dual-process theory and prior verbal memory findings (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006), we expect a quadratic pattern in false alarm rates—specifically, a sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1 as high-fidelity episodic information becomes inaccessible, followed by gradual improvement as familiarity signals weaken. Crucially, we predict that target-lure similarity will amplify this pattern: highly similar lures should produce steeper initial declines and higher peak false alarm rates. This design allows us to map the dynamic interplay between precision decay and similarity-driven familiarity as memory unfolds across continuous experience.
```

toolUseResult.newString:
Beyond temporal factors, discrimination difficulty represents another critical dimension affecting memory precision. MST studies consistently demonstrate that performance deteriorates as target-lure similarity increases, reflecting the mounting challenge of overcoming familiarity signals from highly similar lures (Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). This raises a crucial question: how does lure similarity interact with the temporal decay of mnemonic precision? Evidence from conjunction lure studies suggests a synergistic relationship—as recollection signals weaken over time, participants increasingly rely on familiarity, making similar lures progressively harder to reject (Jones & Atchley, 2006). We hypothesize that this interaction should produce a multiplicative effect: the rapid decay of precision information combined with strong familiarity signals from similar lures should create particularly steep performance declines at short intervals.

The present study directly tests whether lure similarity modulates the temporal decay of mnemonic precision at short intervals. Using a continuous recognition paradigm, we systematically manipulated lag (0-32 intervening items) between initial target presentation and subsequent test with either repetition or similar lure. To quantify target-lure similarity objectively, we developed a novel approach: training a predictive model using feature representations from VGG-16, a deep convolutional neural network (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015), to estimate false alarm probability for each lure based on its perceptual similarity to the target. 

Our central hypothesis predicts a characteristic temporal signature of precision decay. Based on dual-process theory and prior verbal memory findings (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006), we expect a quadratic pattern in false alarm rates—specifically, a sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1 as high-fidelity episodic information becomes inaccessible, followed by gradual improvement as familiarity signals weaken. Crucially, we predict that target-lure similarity will amplify this pattern: highly similar lures should produce steeper initial declines and higher peak false alarm rates. This design allows us to map the dynamic interplay between precision decay and similarity-driven familiarity as memory unfolds across continuous experience.

Rapid Decay of Mnemonic Precision: Evidence from Short-Interval Continuous Recognition

Corey Loo¹,², Bradley Buchsbaum¹,²

¹Rotman Research Institute, Baycrest Health Sciences
Toronto, Ontario M6A 2E1, Canada

²Department of Psychology, University of Toronto
Toronto, Ontario M5S 1A1, Canada

## Abstract

[Note: Abstract is missing in the original manuscript and needs to be written]

## 1. Introduction

Human working memory has a severely limited capacity, typically holding only a few items at once (Cowan, 2001). Consequently, when processing continuous streams of information, working memory contents undergo constant updating. Consider reading a magazine: each new page commands immediate attention while previous pages recede from awareness. Yet we are not entirely anchored to the present moment—memory traces persist and can be retrieved when needed. However, our ability to access these past elements depends critically on both elapsed time and the number of intervening items. As time passes and new experiences accumulate, memories undergo two primary forms of degradation: temporal decay (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; Cowan, Saults, & Nugent, 1997; Hardt, Nader, & Nadel, 2013; Richards & Frankland, 2017) and interference-based corruption (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; García-Rueda, Poch, & Campo, 2022; Wixted, 2004; Yeung, Ryan, Cowell, & Barense, 2013). Both processes progressively diminish memory precision and fidelity.

The temporal dynamics of memory decay during continuous information processing have been extensively characterized using word recognition tasks. In the classic continuous recognition paradigm (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961), participants classify each item in a sequential stream as either "old" (previously seen) or "new" (novel). This paradigm has consistently revealed that recognition accuracy follows an approximately exponential decay function, declining predictably with both elapsed time and intervening items (Buchsbaum, Padmanabhan, & Berman, 2011; Hintzman, 1969; Hockley, 1982; Rubin & Wenzel, 1996). However, standard continuous recognition procedures have a critical limitation: they poorly assess memory precision (Brady, Robinson, & Williams, 2024). The typically low similarity between targets and foils allows participants to succeed using coarse-grained global matching strategies rather than retrieving fine-grained mnemonic details. This methodological constraint has left the temporal evolution of memory precision largely unexplored.

The mnemonic similarity task (MST) provides a powerful paradigm for assessing memory precision with visual stimuli (Kirwan, Jones, Miller, & Stark, 2007; Stark, Kirwan, & Stark, 2019). This task specifically targets "pattern separation"—the neural mechanism that creates distinct memory representations from overlapping perceptual inputs. In its standard study-test format, participants first encode a series of target objects, often through incidental learning. During the subsequent test phase, they must discriminate between three stimulus types: repeated targets, novel foils, and critically, "similar lures" that share perceptual features with the targets. Task difficulty can be systematically manipulated by varying target-lure similarity (Lacy, Yassa, Stark, Muftuler, & Stark, 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). Research consistently demonstrates that false alarm rates increase monotonically with target-lure similarity (Kim & Yassa, 2013; Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012; Stark et al., 2019; Stark, Stevenson, Wu, Rutledge, & Stark, 2015).

Although researchers have developed a continuous recognition variant of the MST (Lacy et al., 2011; Stark et al., 2015; Yassa et al., 2010), existing implementations have notable limitations. By manipulating the lag between initial target presentation and subsequent test (either as repetition or similar lure), this paradigm can track precision changes over time. Previous studies have documented declining hit rates and correct rejections with increasing lag (Ally, Hussey, Ko, & Molitor, 2013; Molitor, Ko, Hussey, & Ally, 2014; Stark et al., 2015). However, these investigations have examined only extended intervals—testing after 4, 12, or 40 intervening items (Ally et al., 2013; Molitor et al., 2014) or sampling from ranges of 0-9, 20-80, or 120-180 items (Stark et al., 2015). This focus on longer delays has left a crucial gap: we do not know how mnemonic precision changes at very short intervals, particularly after just a single intervening item, where working memory processes should still be active.

Parallel investigations using verbal stimuli have revealed intriguing patterns of short-term mnemonic precision decay. Jones and Atchley (2002; 2006) examined this phenomenon using a feature-conjunction paradigm that demands precise verbal memory discrimination. In their continuous recognition task, participants encountered sequences of compound words, some of which were repeated after varying lags (0, 1, 5, 10, or 20 intervening items). Crucially, some repetitions were replaced with conjunction lures—novel compounds created by recombining morphemes from previously studied words (e.g., "blackmail" and "jailbird" recombined to form the lure "blackbird"). These conjunction lures revealed a striking curvilinear pattern of false alarms: an initial sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1, followed by a gradual decline at longer lags. Jones and Atchley interpreted this pattern through dual-process recognition theory (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Jacoby, 1991; Jones, Jacoby, & Gellis, 2001; Mandler, 1980, 1991; Yonelinas, 1994, 1997, 2002), suggesting that the relative decay rates of distinct memory signals produce this characteristic temporal profile.

Dual-process theory posits that recognition memory operates through two distinct mechanisms: recollection and familiarity (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Diana, Yonelinas, & Ranganath, 2010; Yonelinas, 2002). Recollection involves retrieving specific contextual details from the original encoding episode—a precise, high-fidelity process. In contrast, familiarity relies on a global match signal between current perception and stored memory traces, generating a diffuse sense of prior encounter without specific episodic detail. This distinction becomes critical when confronting similar lures: their overlapping features with stored targets trigger strong familiarity signals, increasing false recognition risk. Participants can employ "recollect-to-reject" strategies, using retrieved episodic details to differentiate lures from genuine targets. However, the temporal dynamics strongly favor familiarity. High-precision episodic details decay rapidly from short-term memory (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006, 2008; Matzen, Taylor, & Benjamin, 2011), while familiarity signals persist considerably longer. Consequently, immediate recognition tests allow successful lure rejection through accessible episodic details, but even brief delays shift the balance toward familiarity-based errors as precision information becomes unavailable.

Beyond temporal factors, discrimination difficulty represents another critical dimension affecting memory precision. MST studies consistently demonstrate that performance deteriorates as target-lure similarity increases, reflecting the mounting challenge of overcoming familiarity signals from highly similar lures (Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). This raises a crucial question: how does lure similarity interact with the temporal decay of mnemonic precision? Evidence from conjunction lure studies suggests a synergistic relationship—as recollection signals weaken over time, participants increasingly rely on familiarity, making similar lures progressively harder to reject (Jones & Atchley, 2006). We hypothesize that this interaction should produce a multiplicative effect: the rapid decay of precision information combined with strong familiarity signals from similar lures should create particularly steep performance declines at short intervals.

+Beyond temporal factors, discrimination difficulty represents another critical dimension affecting memory precision. MST studies consistently demonstrate that performance deteriorates as target-lure similarity increases, reflecting the mounting challenge of overcoming familiarity signals from highly similar lures (Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). This raises a crucial question: how does lure similarity interact with the temporal decay of mnemonic precision? Evidence from conjunction lure studies suggests a synergistic relationship—as recollection signals weaken over time, participants increasingly rely on familiarity, making similar lures progressively harder to reject (Jones & Atchley, 2006). We hypothesize that this interaction should produce a multiplicative effect: the rapid decay of precision information combined with strong familiarity signals from similar lures should create particularly steep performance declines at short intervals.

+The present study directly tests whether lure similarity modulates the temporal decay of mnemonic precision at short intervals. Using a continuous recognition paradigm, we systematically manipulated lag (0-32 intervening items) between initial target presentation and subsequent test with either repetition or similar lure. To quantify target-lure similarity objectively, we developed a novel approach: training a predictive model using feature representations from VGG-16, a deep convolutional neural network (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015), to estimate false alarm probability for each lure based on its perceptual similarity to the target.

+Our central hypothesis predicts a characteristic temporal signature of precision decay. Based on dual-process theory and prior verbal memory findings (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006), we expect a quadratic pattern in false alarm rates—specifically, a sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1 as high-fidelity episodic information becomes inaccessible, followed by gradual improvement as familiarity signals weaken. Crucially, we predict that target-lure similarity will amplify this pattern: highly similar lures should produce steeper initial declines and higher peak false alarm rates. This design allows us to map the dynamic interplay between precision decay and similarity-driven familiarity as memory unfolds across continuous experience.

content.text:
## Methods Section

### Methods - Participants

**Original:**
```
Two-hundred and eighty young adults (between the ages of 18-35) residing in Canada, the US, or the UK were recruited through the online participant recruitment platform, Prolific (www.Prolific.com). Participants were filtered using Prolific's pre-screening function and were required to have reported having normal or corrected to normal vision and no history of neurological or psychological disease. Participants were compensated at a rate of 5.36 CAD, 10.72 CAD/hour (3.25 GBP, 6.50 GBP/hour). Eight participants were removed from analysis due to having near-chance performance on target recognition.
```

**Improved:**
```
### 2.1 Participants

We recruited 280 young adults (ages 18-35) from Canada, the United States, and the United Kingdom through Prolific (www.prolific.com), an online participant recruitment platform. Pre-screening criteria ensured all participants had normal or corrected-to-normal vision and no reported history of neurological or psychological disorders. Participants received compensation at £6.50/hour (approximately $10.72 CAD/hour). Eight participants were excluded from analyses for performing at chance level on target recognition trials, yielding a final sample of 272 participants.
```

Crucially, we predict that target-lure similarity will amplify this pattern: highly similar lures should produce steeper initial declines and higher peak false alarm rates. This design allows us to map the dynamic interplay between precision decay and similarity-driven familiarity as memory unfolds across continuous experience.

Crucially, we predict that target-lure similarity will amplify this pattern: highly similar lures should produce steeper initial declines and higher peak false alarm rates. This design allows us to map the dynamic interplay between precision decay and similarity-driven familiarity as memory unfolds across continuous experience.

We recruited 280 young adults (ages 18-35) from Canada, the United States, and the United Kingdom through Prolific (www.prolific.com), an online participant recruitment platform. Participants received compensation at £6.50/hour (approximately $10.72 CAD/hour). Eight participants were excluded from analyses for performing at chance level on target recognition trials, yielding a final sample of 272 participants.

+We recruited 280 young adults (ages 18-35) from Canada, the United States, and the United Kingdom through Prolific (www.prolific.com), an online participant recruitment platform. Pre-screening criteria ensured all participants had normal or corrected-to-normal vision and no reported history of neurological or psychological disorders. Participants received compensation at £6.50/hour (approximately $10.72 CAD/hour). Eight participants were excluded from analyses for performing at chance level on target recognition trials, yielding a final sample of 272 participants.

### Methods - Stimuli

**Original:**
```
Five-hundred complex naturalistic coloured images of varying content (including objects, scenes, animals, and people in motion; see Figure 1 for examples of image pairs) were gathered from online sources. For each image, an image pair was acquired using Google's similar image search function, resulting in a total of 1000 images. Each image was resized to a resolution of 400 by 400 pixels.
 
Figure 1. Sample of similar image pairs. The full inventory consisted of 500 similar image pairs, consisting of images of objects, animals, scenes, and people in motion.
```

**Improved:**
```
### 2.2 Stimuli

We assembled 500 pairs of similar images (1,000 images total) depicting complex naturalistic content including objects, scenes, animals, and people in motion (Figure 1). Target images were sourced from online databases, with corresponding similar images identified using Google's reverse image search functionality. This approach ensured naturally occurring perceptual similarity across diverse stimulus categories. All images were standardized to 400 × 400 pixel resolution and presented in full color.

```

Rapid Decay of Mnemonic Precision: Evidence from Short-Interval Continuous Recognition

Corey Loo¹,², Bradley Buchsbaum¹,²

¹Rotman Research Institute, Baycrest Health Sciences
Toronto, Ontario M6A 2E1, Canada

²Department of Psychology, University of Toronto
Toronto, Ontario M5S 1A1, Canada

## Abstract

[Note: Abstract is missing in the original manuscript and needs to be written]

## 1. Introduction

Human working memory has a severely limited capacity, typically holding only a few items at once (Cowan, 2001). Consequently, when processing continuous streams of information, working memory contents undergo constant updating. Consider reading a magazine: each new page commands immediate attention while previous pages recede from awareness. Yet we are not entirely anchored to the present moment—memory traces persist and can be retrieved when needed. However, our ability to access these past elements depends critically on both elapsed time and the number of intervening items. As time passes and new experiences accumulate, memories undergo two primary forms of degradation: temporal decay (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; Cowan, Saults, & Nugent, 1997; Hardt, Nader, & Nadel, 2013; Richards & Frankland, 2017) and interference-based corruption (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; García-Rueda, Poch, & Campo, 2022; Wixted, 2004; Yeung, Ryan, Cowell, & Barense, 2013). Both processes progressively diminish memory precision and fidelity.

The temporal dynamics of memory decay during continuous information processing have been extensively characterized using word recognition tasks. In the classic continuous recognition paradigm (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961), participants classify each item in a sequential stream as either "old" (previously seen) or "new" (novel). This paradigm has consistently revealed that recognition accuracy follows an approximately exponential decay function, declining predictably with both elapsed time and intervening items (Buchsbaum, Padmanabhan, & Berman, 2011; Hintzman, 1969; Hockley, 1982; Rubin & Wenzel, 1996). However, standard continuous recognition procedures have a critical limitation: they poorly assess memory precision (Brady, Robinson, & Williams, 2024). The typically low similarity between targets and foils allows participants to succeed using coarse-grained global matching strategies rather than retrieving fine-grained mnemonic details. This methodological constraint has left the temporal evolution of memory precision largely unexplored.

The mnemonic similarity task (MST) provides a powerful paradigm for assessing memory precision with visual stimuli (Kirwan, Jones, Miller, & Stark, 2007; Stark, Kirwan, & Stark, 2019). This task specifically targets "pattern separation"—the neural mechanism that creates distinct memory representations from overlapping perceptual inputs. In its standard study-test format, participants first encode a series of target objects, often through incidental learning. During the subsequent test phase, they must discriminate between three stimulus types: repeated targets, novel foils, and critically, "similar lures" that share perceptual features with the targets. Task difficulty can be systematically manipulated by varying target-lure similarity (Lacy, Yassa, Stark, Muftuler, & Stark, 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). Research consistently demonstrates that false alarm rates increase monotonically with target-lure similarity (Kim & Yassa, 2013; Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012; Stark et al., 2019; Stark, Stevenson, Wu, Rutledge, & Stark, 2015).

Although researchers have developed a continuous recognition variant of the MST (Lacy et al., 2011; Stark et al., 2015; Yassa et al., 2010), existing implementations have notable limitations. By manipulating the lag between initial target presentation and subsequent test (either as repetition or similar lure), this paradigm can track precision changes over time. Previous studies have documented declining hit rates and correct rejections with increasing lag (Ally, Hussey, Ko, & Molitor, 2013; Molitor, Ko, Hussey, & Ally, 2014; Stark et al., 2015). However, these investigations have examined only extended intervals—testing after 4, 12, or 40 intervening items (Ally et al., 2013; Molitor et al., 2014) or sampling from ranges of 0-9, 20-80, or 120-180 items (Stark et al., 2015). This focus on longer delays has left a crucial gap: we do not know how mnemonic precision changes at very short intervals, particularly after just a single intervening item, where working memory processes should still be active.

Parallel investigations using verbal stimuli have revealed intriguing patterns of short-term mnemonic precision decay. Jones and Atchley (2002; 2006) examined this phenomenon using a feature-conjunction paradigm that demands precise verbal memory discrimination. In their continuous recognition task, participants encountered sequences of compound words, some of which were repeated after varying lags (0, 1, 5, 10, or 20 intervening items). Crucially, some repetitions were replaced with conjunction lures—novel compounds created by recombining morphemes from previously studied words (e.g., "blackmail" and "jailbird" recombined to form the lure "blackbird"). These conjunction lures revealed a striking curvilinear pattern of false alarms: an initial sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1, followed by a gradual decline at longer lags. Jones and Atchley interpreted this pattern through dual-process recognition theory (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Jacoby, 1991; Jones, Jacoby, & Gellis, 2001; Mandler, 1980, 1991; Yonelinas, 1994, 1997, 2002), suggesting that the relative decay rates of distinct memory signals produce this characteristic temporal profile.

Dual-process theory posits that recognition memory operates through two distinct mechanisms: recollection and familiarity (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Diana, Yonelinas, & Ranganath, 2010; Yonelinas, 2002). Recollection involves retrieving specific contextual details from the original encoding episode—a precise, high-fidelity process. In contrast, familiarity relies on a global match signal between current perception and stored memory traces, generating a diffuse sense of prior encounter without specific episodic detail. This distinction becomes critical when confronting similar lures: their overlapping features with stored targets trigger strong familiarity signals, increasing false recognition risk. Participants can employ "recollect-to-reject" strategies, using retrieved episodic details to differentiate lures from genuine targets. However, the temporal dynamics strongly favor familiarity. High-precision episodic details decay rapidly from short-term memory (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006, 2008; Matzen, Taylor, & Benjamin, 2011), while familiarity signals persist considerably longer. Consequently, immediate recognition tests allow successful lure rejection through accessible episodic details, but even brief delays shift the balance toward familiarity-based errors as precision information becomes unavailable.

Beyond temporal factors, discrimination difficulty represents another critical dimension affecting memory precision. MST studies consistently demonstrate that performance deteriorates as target-lure similarity increases, reflecting the mounting challenge of overcoming familiarity signals from highly similar lures (Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). This raises a crucial question: how does lure similarity interact with the temporal decay of mnemonic precision? Evidence from conjunction lure studies suggests a synergistic relationship—as recollection signals weaken over time, participants increasingly rely on familiarity, making similar lures progressively harder to reject (Jones & Atchley, 2006). We hypothesize that this interaction should produce a multiplicative effect: the rapid decay of precision information combined with strong familiarity signals from similar lures should create particularly steep performance declines at short intervals.

The present study directly tests whether lure similarity modulates the temporal decay of mnemonic precision at short intervals. Using a continuous recognition paradigm, we systematically manipulated lag (0-32 intervening items) between initial target presentation and subsequent test with either repetition or similar lure. To quantify target-lure similarity objectively, we developed a novel approach: training a predictive model using feature representations from VGG-16, a deep convolutional neural network (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015), to estimate false alarm probability for each lure based on its perceptual similarity to the target. 

Our central hypothesis predicts a characteristic temporal signature of precision decay. Based on dual-process theory and prior verbal memory findings (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006), we expect a quadratic pattern in false alarm rates—specifically, a sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1 as high-fidelity episodic information becomes inaccessible, followed by gradual improvement as familiarity signals weaken. Crucially, we predict that target-lure similarity will amplify this pattern: highly similar lures should produce steeper initial declines and higher peak false alarm rates. This design allows us to map the dynamic interplay between precision decay and similarity-driven familiarity as memory unfolds across continuous experience.

## 2. Methods

### 2.1 Participants

We recruited 280 young adults (ages 18-35) from Canada, the United States, and the United Kingdom through Prolific (www.prolific.com), an online participant recruitment platform. Pre-screening criteria ensured all participants had normal or corrected-to-normal vision and no reported history of neurological or psychological disorders. Participants received compensation at £6.50/hour (approximately $10.72 CAD/hour). Eight participants were excluded from analyses for performing at chance level on target recognition trials, yielding a final sample of 272 participants.

content.text:
### Methods - Procedure

**Original:**
```
Participants completed two blocks of a continuous recognition task (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961). On each trial, an image was presented in the center of the screen and participants were required to indicate if the presented image had been seen earlier during the current block. Participants indicated whether the current image was identical to a previously presented image from the current block by pressing the "z" key on the keyboard. If the image had not been seen before in the current block, the "m" key on the keyboard was to be pressed. A given image could only be repeated once throughout the entire study, either presented as a repeated target or a similar lure. Labels indicating which key corresponded to each response were presented below the image to remind participants of which keys to press.
To ensure that sampling time was consistent across all images, the image remained on screen for the first 1.5 seconds. If a response was made during the 1.5 second period that the image was on screen, participants proceeded to the next trial. Otherwise, the image would disappear and participants had up to an additional 1.5 seconds to make a response, before automatically proceeding to the next trial. In total, participants had a 3 second window to respond. Each trial was separated by a 1 second fixation period.
In each block 40 images were repeated during the task, each being presented twice. Another 40 images were repeated, but on the second presentation, a similar lure was presented instead. Thirty images that were not repeated were also included, serving as novel foils. Thus, each block consisted of 190 trials (80 first presentations, 40 second presentations (repeat), 40 second presentations (lure), 30 novel foils). We manipulated lag, the number of intervening items between the first and second presentations of an image (for example a lag 0 would indicate that the second presentation of an object as either a repeat or a lure immediately followed the first presentation), such that there were 8 levels of lag (0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 24, or 32 intervening items).
```

**Improved:**
```
### 2.3 Procedure

Participants completed two blocks of a continuous recognition task (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961). Each trial presented a single image centrally for 1.5 seconds. Participants judged whether each image was identical to one seen previously in the current block, responding "old" (z key) or "new" (m key). Response reminders appeared below each image. If participants responded during the initial 1.5-second presentation, the trial advanced immediately; otherwise, the image disappeared and participants had an additional 1.5 seconds to respond (3-second total response window). A 1-second fixation cross separated trials.

Each block contained 190 trials with the following composition:
- 80 initial presentations (targets)
- 40 exact repetitions of targets
- 40 similar lures (perceptually similar to targets but not identical)
- 30 novel foils (never repeated)

Each image appeared at most twice per experiment, either as an exact repetition or replaced by its similar lure. We systematically manipulated lag—the number of intervening items between initial presentation and test—across eight levels: 0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 24, and 32 items. This design yielded 10 observations per participant for each combination of trial type (repeat/lure) and lag.
```

We recruited 280 young adults (ages 18-35) from Canada, the United States, and the United Kingdom through Prolific (www.prolific.com), an online participant recruitment platform. Pre-screening criteria ensured all participants had normal or corrected-to-normal vision and no reported history of neurological or psychological disorders. Participants received compensation at £6.50/hour (approximately $10.72 CAD/hour). Eight participants were excluded from analyses for performing at chance level on target recognition trials, yielding a final sample of 272 participants.

### 2.2 Stimuli

We assembled 500 pairs of similar images (1,000 images total) depicting complex naturalistic content including objects, scenes, animals, and people in motion (Figure 1). Target images were sourced from online databases, with corresponding similar images identified using Google's reverse image search functionality. This approach ensured naturally occurring perceptual similarity across diverse stimulus categories. All images were standardized to 400 × 400 pixel resolution and presented in full color.

[Figure 1. Representative examples of target-lure image pairs. The stimulus set comprised 500 such pairs spanning diverse content categories including objects, animals, scenes, and human actions.]

+### 2.3 Procedure

+Participants completed two blocks of a continuous recognition task (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961). Each trial presented a single image centrally for 1.5 seconds. Participants judged whether each image was identical to one seen previously in the current block, responding "old" (z key) or "new" (m key). Response reminders appeared below each image.
