toolUseResult.originalFile:
Rapid Decay of Mnemonic Precision: Evidence from Short-Interval Continuous Recognition

Corey Loo¹,², Bradley Buchsbaum¹,²

¹Rotman Research Institute, Baycrest Health Sciences
Toronto, Ontario M6A 2E1, Canada

²Department of Psychology, University of Toronto
Toronto, Ontario M5S 1A1, Canada

## Abstract

How quickly does memory precision decay when confronted with similar information? Using a continuous recognition paradigm with naturalistic images, we investigated the temporal dynamics of mnemonic precision by systematically varying the lag (0-32 intervening items) between initial presentation and subsequent test with either identical repetitions or similar lures. We quantified target-lure similarity using a deep learning model trained to predict behavioral confusability. Results revealed a striking non-monotonic pattern: lure discrimination was exceptional at lag 0, declined catastrophically by lag 1, then gradually recovered at longer intervals. Critically, this temporal signature was amplified by target-lure similarity—highly similar lures produced significantly steeper declines between lag 0 and 1. These findings demonstrate that precise episodic details become inaccessible within seconds of encoding, leaving recognition increasingly dependent on familiarity signals that similar lures readily trigger. The interaction between similarity and time reveals fundamental constraints on how working memory maintains high-fidelity representations during continuous experience. Our results bridge research on pattern separation, dual-process recognition theory, and working memory architecture, suggesting that the transition from focused attention fundamentally alters the resolution of mnemonic representations.

## 1. Introduction

Human working memory has a severely limited capacity, typically holding only a few items at once (Cowan, 2001). Consequently, when processing continuous streams of information, working memory contents undergo constant updating. Consider reading a magazine: each new page commands immediate attention while previous pages recede from awareness. Yet we are not entirely anchored to the present moment—memory traces persist and can be retrieved when needed. However, our ability to access these past elements depends critically on both elapsed time and the number of intervening items. As time passes and new experiences accumulate, memories undergo two primary forms of degradation: temporal decay (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; Cowan, Saults, & Nugent, 1997; Hardt, Nader, & Nadel, 2013; Richards & Frankland, 2017) and interference-based corruption (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; García-Rueda, Poch, & Campo, 2022; Wixted, 2004; Yeung, Ryan, Cowell, & Barense, 2013). Both processes progressively diminish memory precision and fidelity.

The temporal dynamics of memory decay during continuous information processing have been extensively characterized using word recognition tasks. In the classic continuous recognition paradigm (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961), participants classify each item in a sequential stream as either "old" (previously seen) or "new" (novel). This paradigm has consistently revealed that recognition accuracy follows an approximately exponential decay function, declining predictably with both elapsed time and intervening items (Buchsbaum, Padmanabhan, & Berman, 2011; Hintzman, 1969; Hockley, 1982; Rubin & Wenzel, 1996). However, standard continuous recognition procedures have a critical limitation: they poorly assess memory precision (Brady, Robinson, & Williams, 2024). The typically low similarity between targets and foils allows participants to succeed using coarse-grained global matching strategies rather than retrieving fine-grained mnemonic details. This methodological constraint has left the temporal evolution of memory precision largely unexplored.

The mnemonic similarity task (MST) provides a powerful paradigm for assessing memory precision with visual stimuli (Kirwan, Jones, Miller, & Stark, 2007; Stark, Kirwan, & Stark, 2019). This task specifically targets "pattern separation"—the neural mechanism that creates distinct memory representations from overlapping perceptual inputs. In its standard study-test format, participants first encode a series of target objects, often through incidental learning. During the subsequent test phase, they must discriminate between three stimulus types: repeated targets, novel foils, and critically, "similar lures" that share perceptual features with the targets. Task difficulty can be systematically manipulated by varying target-lure similarity (Lacy, Yassa, Stark, Muftuler, & Stark, 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). Research consistently demonstrates that false alarm rates increase monotonically with target-lure similarity (Kim & Yassa, 2013; Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012; Stark et al., 2019; Stark, Stevenson, Wu, Rutledge, & Stark, 2015).

Although researchers have developed a continuous recognition variant of the MST (Lacy et al., 2011; Stark et al., 2015; Yassa et al., 2010), existing implementations have notable limitations. By manipulating the lag between initial target presentation and subsequent test (either as repetition or similar lure), this paradigm can track precision changes over time. Previous studies have documented declining hit rates and correct rejections with increasing lag (Ally, Hussey, Ko, & Molitor, 2013; Molitor, Ko, Hussey, & Ally, 2014; Stark et al., 2015). However, these investigations have examined only extended intervals—testing after 4, 12, or 40 intervening items (Ally et al., 2013; Molitor et al., 2014) or sampling from ranges of 0-9, 20-80, or 120-180 items (Stark et al., 2015). This focus on longer delays has left a crucial gap: we do not know how mnemonic precision changes at very short intervals, particularly after just a single intervening item, where working memory processes should still be active.

Parallel investigations using verbal stimuli have revealed intriguing patterns of short-term mnemonic precision decay. Jones and Atchley (2002; 2006) examined this phenomenon using a feature-conjunction paradigm that demands precise verbal memory discrimination. In their continuous recognition task, participants encountered sequences of compound words, some of which were repeated after varying lags (0, 1, 5, 10, or 20 intervening items). Crucially, some repetitions were replaced with conjunction lures—novel compounds created by recombining morphemes from previously studied words (e.g., "blackmail" and "jailbird" recombined to form the lure "blackbird"). These conjunction lures revealed a striking curvilinear pattern of false alarms: an initial sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1, followed by a gradual decline at longer lags. Jones and Atchley interpreted this pattern through dual-process recognition theory (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Jacoby, 1991; Jones, Jacoby, & Gellis, 2001; Mandler, 1980, 1991; Yonelinas, 1994, 1997, 2002), suggesting that the relative decay rates of distinct memory signals produce this characteristic temporal profile.

Dual-process theory posits that recognition memory operates through two distinct mechanisms: recollection and familiarity (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Diana, Yonelinas, & Ranganath, 2010; Yonelinas, 2002). Recollection involves retrieving specific contextual details from the original encoding episode—a precise, high-fidelity process. In contrast, familiarity relies on a global match signal between current perception and stored memory traces, generating a diffuse sense of prior encounter without specific episodic detail. This distinction becomes critical when confronting similar lures: their overlapping features with stored targets trigger strong familiarity signals, increasing false recognition risk. Participants can employ "recollect-to-reject" strategies, using retrieved episodic details to differentiate lures from genuine targets. However, the temporal dynamics strongly favor familiarity. High-precision episodic details decay rapidly from short-term memory (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006, 2008; Matzen, Taylor, & Benjamin, 2011), while familiarity signals persist considerably longer. Consequently, immediate recognition tests allow successful lure rejection through accessible episodic details, but even brief delays shift the balance toward familiarity-based errors as precision information becomes unavailable.

Beyond temporal factors, discrimination difficulty represents another critical dimension affecting memory precision. MST studies consistently demonstrate that performance deteriorates as target-lure similarity increases, reflecting the mounting challenge of overcoming familiarity signals from highly similar lures (Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). This raises a crucial question: how does lure similarity interact with the temporal decay of mnemonic precision? Evidence from conjunction lure studies suggests a synergistic relationship—as recollection signals weaken over time, participants increasingly rely on familiarity, making similar lures progressively harder to reject (Jones & Atchley, 2006). We hypothesize that this interaction should produce a multiplicative effect: the rapid decay of precision information combined with strong familiarity signals from similar lures should create particularly steep performance declines at short intervals.

The present study directly tests whether lure similarity modulates the temporal decay of mnemonic precision at short intervals. Using a continuous recognition paradigm, we systematically manipulated lag (0-32 intervening items) between initial target presentation and subsequent test with either repetition or similar lure. To quantify target-lure similarity objectively, we developed a novel approach: training a predictive model using feature representations from VGG-16, a deep convolutional neural network (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015), to estimate false alarm probability for each lure based on its perceptual similarity to the target. 

Our central hypothesis predicts a characteristic temporal signature of precision decay. Based on dual-process theory and prior verbal memory findings (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006), we expect a quadratic pattern in false alarm rates—specifically, a sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1 as high-fidelity episodic information becomes inaccessible, followed by gradual improvement as familiarity signals weaken. Crucially, we predict that target-lure similarity will amplify this pattern: highly similar lures should produce steeper initial declines and higher peak false alarm rates. This design allows us to map the dynamic interplay between precision decay and similarity-driven familiarity as memory unfolds across continuous experience.

## 2. Methods

### 2.1 Participants

We recruited 280 young adults (ages 18-35) from Canada, the United States, and the United Kingdom through Prolific (www.prolific.com), an online participant recruitment platform. Pre-screening criteria ensured all participants had normal or corrected-to-normal vision and no reported history of neurological or psychological disorders. Participants received compensation at £6.50/hour (approximately $10.72 CAD/hour). Eight participants were excluded from analyses for performing at chance level on target recognition trials, yielding a final sample of 272 participants.

### 2.2 Stimuli

We assembled 500 pairs of similar images (1,000 images total) depicting complex naturalistic content including objects, scenes, animals, and people in motion (Figure 1). Target images were sourced from online databases, with corresponding similar images identified using Google's reverse image search functionality. This approach ensured naturally occurring perceptual similarity across diverse stimulus categories. All images were standardized to 400 × 400 pixel resolution and presented in full color.

[Figure 1. Representative examples of target-lure image pairs. The stimulus set comprised 500 such pairs spanning diverse content categories including objects, animals, scenes, and human actions.]

### 2.3 Procedure

Participants completed two blocks of a continuous recognition task (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961). Each trial presented a single image centrally for 1.5 seconds. Participants judged whether each image was identical to one seen previously in the current block, responding "old" (z key) or "new" (m key). Response reminders appeared below each image. If participants responded during the initial 1.5-second presentation, the trial advanced immediately; otherwise, the image disappeared and participants had an additional 1.5 seconds to respond (3-second total response window). A 1-second fixation cross separated trials.

Each block contained 190 trials with the following composition:
- 80 initial presentations (targets)
- 40 exact repetitions of targets
- 40 similar lures (perceptually similar to targets but not identical)
- 30 novel foils (never repeated)

Each image appeared at most twice per experiment, either as an exact repetition or replaced by its similar lure. We systematically manipulated lag—the number of intervening items between initial presentation and test—across eight levels: 0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 24, and 32 items. This design yielded 10 observations per participant for each combination of trial type (repeat/lure) and lag.

### 2.4 Similarity Metric

To quantify target-lure similarity objectively, we leveraged deep learning feature representations from VGG-16 (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015), a 23-layer convolutional neural network pre-trained on ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009). For each image pair, we extracted activation patterns from all network layers, creating feature vectors that capture visual processing from low-level edges through high-level semantic content.

Following Bone et al. (2020), we grouped the 23 layers into hierarchical feature clusters representing low-level visual features in early layers, mid-level visual features, high-level visual features, and semantic features in late layers. We computed cosine similarity between target and lure representations within each cluster, yielding five similarity metrics per image pair.

To predict behavioral confusability, we trained a LASSO-penalized logistic regression model (Tibshirani, 1996) using the glmnet package (Friedman, Hastie, & Tibshirani, 2010). The model predicted false alarm probability for each lure based on its similarity metrics. We optimized regularization parameters through 20-fold cross-validation, partitioning images into test sets of 25 pairs each.

Model validation revealed 72.7% classification accuracy in predicting participant responses (false alarm vs. correct rejection), confirming that our similarity metrics captured behaviorally relevant perceptual confusability. For analysis, we divided image pairs into tertiles based on predicted false alarm probability: low (Bin 1), medium (Bin 2), and high (Bin 3) confusability.

## 3. Results

Overall recognition performance confirmed participants' engagement with the task. Participants accurately identified repeated targets (M = 0.877, SD = 0.11), performing well above chance (t(271) = 58.31, p < .001, 95% CI [0.865, 0.890]). Critically, participants also successfully discriminated similar lures from targets (M = 0.720, SD = 0.11), again exceeding chance levels (t(271) = 34.85, p < .001, 95% CI [0.710, 0.735]). This pattern indicates that participants maintained sufficient mnemonic precision to distinguish perceptually similar items, validating our paradigm for examining precision decay.

To examine temporal effects on recognition accuracy, we analyzed performance across lag conditions using linear mixed-effects models (lme4: Bates, Mächler, Bolker, & Walker, 2015; lmerTest: Kuznetsova, Brockhoff, & Christensen, 2017). For repeated targets (Figure 2a), we included lag and similarity bin as fixed effects with participant random intercepts. Likelihood ratio tests confirmed significant effects of lag (χ²(2) = 71.86, p < .001), with performance declining as lag increased (β = -0.001, SE = 0.0004). Similarly, confusability bin significantly affected performance (χ²(2) = 1143, p < .001), with higher confusability bins showing reduced accuracy (β = -0.005, SE = 0.0001). These findings demonstrate that both temporal delay and perceptual similarity influence recognition memory, even for identical repetitions.

[Figure 2. Recognition performance as a function of lag and target-lure similarity. (a) Hit rates for repeated targets decline gradually with increasing lag. (b) Correct rejection rates for similar lures show a pronounced quadratic pattern, with sharp declines from lag 0 to lag 1 followed by gradual recovery. Data are stratified by model-predicted confusability tertiles: Bin 1 (low confusability), Bin 2 (medium confusability), and Bin 3 (high confusability). Error bars represent ±1 SEM.]

Our central hypothesis concerned the temporal dynamics of lure discrimination. Visual inspection of lure trial performance (Figure 2b) revealed the predicted curvilinear pattern: an initial sharp decline followed by gradual recovery. To test this formally, we compared mixed-effects models with linear versus quadratic lag terms. The quadratic model provided significantly better fit (χ²(5) = 28.78, p < .001), confirming that lure discrimination follows a non-monotonic trajectory over time. This quadratic pattern—characterized by rapid precision loss at minimal delays followed by gradual improvement—aligns with theoretical predictions about the differential decay rates of recollection and familiarity signals.

Crucially, we tested whether target-lure similarity modulated the temporal pattern of precision decay. Including the lag × confusability interaction significantly improved model fit (χ²(1) = 8.33, p = .004), confirming that similarity amplifies the temporal dynamics. Follow-up contrasts using emmeans (Searle, Speed, & Milliken, 1980) quantified this interaction by examining the lag 0 to lag 1 decline across confusability bins. The performance drop was significantly steeper for high-confusability lures (Δ = .095, SE = .031, 95% CI [.034, .155], t(6179) = 3.08, p = .002) and medium-confusability lures (Δ = .081, SE = .031, 95% CI [.021, .142], t(6179) = 2.64, p = .008) compared to low-confusability lures. High and medium confusability bins did not differ significantly (Δ = .014, SE = .031, 95% CI [-.047, .074], t(6181) = 0.44, p = .662). These results demonstrate that perceptual similarity specifically amplifies the rapid initial decay of mnemonic precision, supporting our hypothesis of a synergistic interaction between similarity-driven familiarity and temporal precision loss.

## 4. Discussion

This study provides the first systematic investigation of mnemonic precision decay at very short intervals using naturalistic visual stimuli. By adapting the continuous MST to include minimal lags, we revealed a striking temporal signature of precision loss that emerges within seconds of encoding. Our findings support three key conclusions. First, false alarm rates increased monotonically with target-lure similarity, validating our neural network-based similarity metric and replicating established MST findings. Second, lure discrimination followed a distinctive quadratic trajectory: exceptional precision at lag 0, catastrophic decline by lag 1, followed by gradual recovery at longer intervals. Third, and most critically, target-lure similarity amplified this temporal pattern—highly similar lures produced steeper initial declines in discrimination accuracy. This interaction between similarity and time reveals how rapidly precise episodic details become inaccessible, leaving recognition increasingly dependent on familiarity signals that similar lures readily trigger.

Our results contribute to understanding the computational mechanisms underlying pattern separation and pattern completion in human memory (Stark et al., 2019). Pattern separation—the ability to encode distinct representations from overlapping inputs—enables successful lure rejection by maintaining high-precision mnemonic traces. Conversely, pattern completion manifests as false recognition when partial cues trigger retrieval of similar stored representations (Yassa et al., 2011). Our finding that false alarm rates scale with target-lure similarity aligns with this framework: as perceptual overlap increases, shared features act as increasingly potent retrieval cues (Hunsaker & Kesner, 2013). This creates a computational tension—highly similar lures activate strong pattern completion signals that must be overcome through pattern separation. Our temporal findings suggest this balance shifts dramatically within seconds: at lag 0, pattern separation dominates, enabling precise discrimination; by lag 1, the loss of detailed representations tips the balance toward pattern completion, particularly for similar lures where overlapping features provide the strongest retrieval cues.

This study addresses a critical gap in the memory literature by systematically examining precision decay at minimal intervals. Previous continuous MST studies have focused on extended lags: Stark et al. (2015) tested intervals from 0-180 items to examine age-related interference effects, while Molitor and colleagues used 4, 12, and 40-item lags to characterize forgetting in clinical populations (Ally et al., 2013; Molitor et al., 2014). These studies consistently report linear declines in both target recognition and lure rejection, suggesting progressive shifts toward pattern completion. However, by excluding short intervals, they missed the dramatic non-monotonic dynamics we observed. The conventional assumption—that working memory maintenance ensures high precision at brief delays—proves only partially correct. While lag 0 performance confirms exceptional precision when items remain in active maintenance, the precipitous decline by lag 1 reveals that this precision advantage is remarkably fragile. This finding challenges simple capacity-based accounts of working memory and suggests that precision loss begins almost immediately as attention shifts to new inputs.

The quadratic pattern we observed parallels findings from verbal memory studies using conjunction lures (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006; Matzen et al., 2011), suggesting common principles govern precision decay across stimulus domains. Dual-process theory provides a compelling framework for understanding these dynamics (Jacoby, 1991; Yonelinas, 1994, 2002). According to this account, recognition operates through two dissociable processes with distinct temporal profiles: recollection of specific episodic details decays rapidly, while familiarity based on global similarity persists longer. At lag 0, participants can employ "recollect-to-reject" strategies, accessing detailed episodic traces to differentiate lures from targets. This precise comparison mechanism fails catastrophically by lag 1 as episodic details become inaccessible, leaving participants vulnerable to familiarity-based false recognition. The subsequent recovery at longer lags reflects weakening familiarity signals as both target and lure representations fade. Our similarity manipulation provides crucial evidence for this dual-process interpretation: highly similar lures evoke stronger familiarity signals that become particularly problematic when recollection fails, producing the steeper lag 0-1 decline we observed for high-similarity pairs.

The relationship between dual-process mechanisms and pattern separation remains contentious in the literature. While Kim and Yassa (2013) found that correct lure rejections elicited more "remember" than "know" responses in a remember/know MST variant, suggesting recollection supports pattern separation, subsequent findings have been inconsistent. Some studies implicate familiarity-based processes (Szőllősi, Bencze, & Racsmány, 2020), while others support recollection's primacy (Molitor et al., 2014; Schaeffer & Chek, 2022). Our temporal findings offer a potential resolution to this debate: the relative contributions of recollection and familiarity to pattern separation may depend critically on timing. At lag 0, successful discrimination likely relies on recollection-based comparisons between detailed representations. However, as these details rapidly decay, the system must increasingly rely on whatever signals remain available—including familiarity-based pattern separation mechanisms that may operate on coarser representations. This dynamic interplay suggests that pattern separation is not exclusively tied to either process but rather reflects the optimal use of available mnemonic information at each time point.

Our findings align remarkably well with Oberauer's concentric model of working memory (Oberauer, 2002, 2006, 2009), which proposes three hierarchical representational states: the focus of attention (single item, highest precision), the region of direct access (3-4 items, moderate precision), and activated long-term memory (unlimited capacity, lowest precision). The exceptional discrimination performance at lag 0 reflects the privileged status of the focus of attention. When a lure immediately follows its target, the target remains in the focus during the transition between trials, enabling high-fidelity comparisons. This transitional overlap—where the previous item briefly persists in focus while the new item is encoded—provides a unique opportunity for precise discrimination.

The catastrophic precision loss by lag 1 marks the transition from focused to peripheral representation. Once displaced from focus, the target moves to the region of direct access or activated long-term memory, where representations lack the resolution necessary for fine-grained discrimination. This architectural constraint explains why even a single intervening item fundamentally alters recognition dynamics. Moreover, the interaction with similarity suggests that transitions between representational states differentially affect various levels of detail: while coarse features supporting familiarity may survive the transition, the fine-grained details required to reject similar lures are selectively lost. This hierarchical degradation of precision as items move through working memory states provides a mechanistic account of our behavioral findings.

In conclusion, our study reveals that mnemonic precision follows a non-monotonic trajectory characterized by catastrophic early decline and gradual recovery. The sharp performance drop between lag 0 and lag 1 demonstrates that precision loss begins almost immediately as attention shifts to new inputs, challenging assumptions about working memory's protective effects. Crucially, this temporal dynamic is amplified by perceptual similarity, with highly similar lures producing steeper initial declines—evidence for a synergistic interaction between similarity-driven familiarity and time-dependent precision loss.

Several important questions remain. Our design cannot disentangle whether precision loss reflects temporal decay, interference from intervening items, or both (Hardt et al., 2013; Sadeh, Ozubko, Winocur, & Moscovitch, 2014). Future studies could vary presentation timing while holding item number constant to isolate these mechanisms. Additionally, investigating individual differences in precision maintenance could reveal whether the lag 0-1 transition represents a fundamental constraint or a flexible boundary. Finally, neuroimaging approaches could test whether the behavioral transitions we observed correspond to shifts in hippocampal pattern separation versus cortical familiarity signals. These findings advance our understanding of how quickly fine-grained memory representations become inaccessible in continuous experience.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
Rapid Decay of Mnemonic Precision: Evidence from Short-Interval Continuous Recognition

Corey Loo¹,², Bradley Buchsbaum¹,²

¹Rotman Research Institute, Baycrest Health Sciences
Toronto, Ontario M6A 2E1, Canada

²Department of Psychology, University of Toronto
Toronto, Ontario M5S 1A1, Canada

## Abstract

How quickly does memory precision decay when confronted with similar information? Using a continuous recognition paradigm with naturalistic images, we investigated the temporal dynamics of mnemonic precision by systematically varying the lag (0-32 intervening items) between initial presentation and subsequent test with either identical repetitions or similar lures. We quantified target-lure similarity using a deep learning model trained to predict behavioral confusability. Results revealed a striking non-monotonic pattern: lure discrimination was exceptional at lag 0, declined catastrophically by lag 1, then gradually recovered at longer intervals. Critically, this temporal signature was amplified by target-lure similarity—highly similar lures produced significantly steeper declines between lag 0 and 1. These findings demonstrate that precise episodic details become inaccessible within seconds of encoding, leaving recognition increasingly dependent on familiarity signals that similar lures readily trigger. The interaction between similarity and time reveals fundamental constraints on how working memory maintains high-fidelity representations during continuous experience. Our results bridge research on pattern separation, dual-process recognition theory, and working memory architecture, suggesting that the transition from focused attention fundamentally alters the resolution of mnemonic representations.

## 1. Introduction

Human working memory has a severely limited capacity, typically holding only a few items at once (Cowan, 2001). Consequently, when processing continuous streams of information, working memory contents undergo constant updating. Consider reading a magazine: each new page commands immediate attention while previous pages recede from awareness. Yet we are not entirely anchored to the present moment—memory traces persist and can be retrieved when needed. However, our ability to access these past elements depends critically on both elapsed time and the number of intervening items. As time passes and new experiences accumulate, memories undergo two primary forms of degradation: temporal decay (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; Cowan, Saults, & Nugent, 1997; Hardt, Nader, & Nadel, 2013; Richards & Frankland, 2017) and interference-based corruption (Altmann & Schunn, 2012; García-Rueda, Poch, & Campo, 2022; Wixted, 2004; Yeung, Ryan, Cowell, & Barense, 2013). Both processes progressively diminish memory precision and fidelity.

The temporal dynamics of memory decay during continuous information processing have been extensively characterized using word recognition tasks. In the classic continuous recognition paradigm (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961), participants classify each item in a sequential stream as either "old" (previously seen) or "new" (novel). This paradigm has consistently revealed that recognition accuracy follows an approximately exponential decay function, declining predictably with both elapsed time and intervening items (Buchsbaum, Padmanabhan, & Berman, 2011; Hintzman, 1969; Hockley, 1982; Rubin & Wenzel, 1996). However, standard continuous recognition procedures have a critical limitation: they poorly assess memory precision (Brady, Robinson, & Williams, 2024). The typically low similarity between targets and foils allows participants to succeed using coarse-grained global matching strategies rather than retrieving fine-grained mnemonic details. This methodological constraint has left the temporal evolution of memory precision largely unexplored.

The mnemonic similarity task (MST) provides a powerful paradigm for assessing memory precision with visual stimuli (Kirwan, Jones, Miller, & Stark, 2007; Stark, Kirwan, & Stark, 2019). This task specifically targets "pattern separation"—the neural mechanism that creates distinct memory representations from overlapping perceptual inputs. In its standard study-test format, participants first encode a series of target objects, often through incidental learning. During the subsequent test phase, they must discriminate between three stimulus types: repeated targets, novel foils, and critically, "similar lures" that share perceptual features with the targets. Task difficulty can be systematically manipulated by varying target-lure similarity (Lacy, Yassa, Stark, Muftuler, & Stark, 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). Research consistently demonstrates that false alarm rates increase monotonically with target-lure similarity (Kim & Yassa, 2013; Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012; Stark et al., 2019; Stark, Stevenson, Wu, Rutledge, & Stark, 2015).

Although researchers have developed a continuous recognition variant of the MST (Lacy et al., 2011; Stark et al., 2015; Yassa et al., 2010), existing implementations have notable limitations. By manipulating the lag between initial target presentation and subsequent test (either as repetition or similar lure), this paradigm can track precision changes over time. Previous studies have documented declining hit rates and correct rejections with increasing lag (Ally, Hussey, Ko, & Molitor, 2013; Molitor, Ko, Hussey, & Ally, 2014; Stark et al., 2015). However, these investigations have examined only extended intervals—testing after 4, 12, or 40 intervening items (Ally et al., 2013; Molitor et al., 2014) or sampling from ranges of 0-9, 20-80, or 120-180 items (Stark et al., 2015). This focus on longer delays has left a crucial gap: we do not know how mnemonic precision changes at very short intervals, particularly after just a single intervening item, where working memory processes should still be active.

Parallel investigations using verbal stimuli have revealed intriguing patterns of short-term mnemonic precision decay. Jones and Atchley (2002; 2006) examined this phenomenon using a feature-conjunction paradigm that demands precise verbal memory discrimination. In their continuous recognition task, participants encountered sequences of compound words, some of which were repeated after varying lags (0, 1, 5, 10, or 20 intervening items). Crucially, some repetitions were replaced with conjunction lures—novel compounds created by recombining morphemes from previously studied words (e.g., "blackmail" and "jailbird" recombined to form the lure "blackbird"). These conjunction lures revealed a striking curvilinear pattern of false alarms: an initial sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1, followed by a gradual decline at longer lags. Jones and Atchley interpreted this pattern through dual-process recognition theory (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Jacoby, 1991; Jones, Jacoby, & Gellis, 2001; Mandler, 1980, 1991; Yonelinas, 1994, 1997, 2002), suggesting that the relative decay rates of distinct memory signals produce this characteristic temporal profile.

Dual-process theory posits that recognition memory operates through two distinct mechanisms: recollection and familiarity (Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Diana, Yonelinas, & Ranganath, 2010; Yonelinas, 2002). Recollection involves retrieving specific contextual details from the original encoding episode—a precise, high-fidelity process. In contrast, familiarity relies on a global match signal between current perception and stored memory traces, generating a diffuse sense of prior encounter without specific episodic detail. This distinction becomes critical when confronting similar lures: their overlapping features with stored targets trigger strong familiarity signals, increasing false recognition risk. Participants can employ "recollect-to-reject" strategies, using retrieved episodic details to differentiate lures from genuine targets. However, the temporal dynamics strongly favor familiarity. High-precision episodic details decay rapidly from short-term memory (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006, 2008; Matzen, Taylor, & Benjamin, 2011), while familiarity signals persist considerably longer. Consequently, immediate recognition tests allow successful lure rejection through accessible episodic details, but even brief delays shift the balance toward familiarity-based errors as precision information becomes unavailable.

Beyond temporal factors, discrimination difficulty represents another critical dimension affecting memory precision. MST studies consistently demonstrate that performance deteriorates as target-lure similarity increases, reflecting the mounting challenge of overcoming familiarity signals from highly similar lures (Lacy et al., 2011; Motley & Brock Kirwan, 2012). This raises a crucial question: how does lure similarity interact with the temporal decay of mnemonic precision? Evidence from conjunction lure studies suggests a synergistic relationship—as recollection signals weaken over time, participants increasingly rely on familiarity, making similar lures progressively harder to reject (Jones & Atchley, 2006). We hypothesize that this interaction should produce a multiplicative effect: the rapid decay of precision information combined with strong familiarity signals from similar lures should create particularly steep performance declines at short intervals.

The present study directly tests whether lure similarity modulates the temporal decay of mnemonic precision at short intervals. Using a continuous recognition paradigm, we systematically manipulated lag (0-32 intervening items) between initial target presentation and subsequent test with either repetition or similar lure. To quantify target-lure similarity objectively, we developed a novel approach: training a predictive model using feature representations from VGG-16, a deep convolutional neural network (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015), to estimate false alarm probability for each lure based on its perceptual similarity to the target. 

Our central hypothesis predicts a characteristic temporal signature of precision decay. Based on dual-process theory and prior verbal memory findings (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006), we expect a quadratic pattern in false alarm rates—specifically, a sharp increase from lag 0 to lag 1 as high-fidelity episodic information becomes inaccessible, followed by gradual improvement as familiarity signals weaken. Crucially, we predict that target-lure similarity will amplify this pattern: highly similar lures should produce steeper initial declines and higher peak false alarm rates. This design allows us to map the dynamic interplay between precision decay and similarity-driven familiarity as memory unfolds across continuous experience.

## 2. Methods

### 2.1 Participants

We recruited 280 young adults (ages 18-35) from Canada, the United States, and the United Kingdom through Prolific (www.prolific.com), an online participant recruitment platform. Pre-screening criteria ensured all participants had normal or corrected-to-normal vision and no reported history of neurological or psychological disorders. Participants received compensation at £6.50/hour (approximately $10.72 CAD/hour). Eight participants were excluded from analyses for performing at chance level on target recognition trials, yielding a final sample of 272 participants.

### 2.2 Stimuli

We assembled 500 pairs of similar images (1,000 images total) depicting complex naturalistic content including objects, scenes, animals, and people in motion (Figure 1). Target images were sourced from online databases, with corresponding similar images identified using Google's reverse image search functionality. This approach ensured naturally occurring perceptual similarity across diverse stimulus categories. All images were standardized to 400 × 400 pixel resolution and presented in full color.

![Figure 1. Representative examples of target-lure image pairs. The stimulus set comprised 500 such pairs spanning diverse content categories including objects, animals, scenes, and human actions.](Figure1.png)

### 2.3 Procedure

Participants completed two blocks of a continuous recognition task (Shepard & Teghtsoonian, 1961). Each trial presented a single image centrally for 1.5 seconds. Participants judged whether each image was identical to one seen previously in the current block, responding "old" (z key) or "new" (m key). Response reminders appeared below each image. If participants responded during the initial 1.5-second presentation, the trial advanced immediately; otherwise, the image disappeared and participants had an additional 1.5 seconds to respond (3-second total response window). A 1-second fixation cross separated trials.

Each block contained 190 trials with the following composition:
- 80 initial presentations (targets)
- 40 exact repetitions of targets
- 40 similar lures (perceptually similar to targets but not identical)
- 30 novel foils (never repeated)

Each image appeared at most twice per experiment, either as an exact repetition or replaced by its similar lure. We systematically manipulated lag—the number of intervening items between initial presentation and test—across eight levels: 0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 24, and 32 items. This design yielded 10 observations per participant for each combination of trial type (repeat/lure) and lag.

### 2.4 Similarity Metric

To quantify target-lure similarity objectively, we leveraged deep learning feature representations from VGG-16 (Simonyan & Zisserman, 2015), a 23-layer convolutional neural network pre-trained on ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009). For each image pair, we extracted activation patterns from all network layers, creating feature vectors that capture visual processing from low-level edges through high-level semantic content.

Following Bone et al. (2020), we grouped the 23 layers into hierarchical feature clusters representing low-level visual features in early layers, mid-level visual features, high-level visual features, and semantic features in late layers. We computed cosine similarity between target and lure representations within each cluster, yielding five similarity metrics per image pair.

To predict behavioral confusability, we trained a LASSO-penalized logistic regression model (Tibshirani, 1996) using the glmnet package (Friedman, Hastie, & Tibshirani, 2010). The model predicted false alarm probability for each lure based on its similarity metrics. We optimized regularization parameters through 20-fold cross-validation, partitioning images into test sets of 25 pairs each.

Model validation revealed 72.7% classification accuracy in predicting participant responses (false alarm vs. correct rejection), confirming that our similarity metrics captured behaviorally relevant perceptual confusability. For analysis, we divided image pairs into tertiles based on predicted false alarm probability: low (Bin 1), medium (Bin 2), and high (Bin 3) confusability.

## 3. Results

Overall recognition performance confirmed participants' engagement with the task. Participants accurately identified repeated targets (M = 0.877, SD = 0.11), performing well above chance (t(271) = 58.31, p < .001, 95% CI [0.865, 0.890]). Critically, participants also successfully discriminated similar lures from targets (M = 0.720, SD = 0.11), again exceeding chance levels (t(271) = 34.85, p < .001, 95% CI [0.710, 0.735]). This pattern indicates that participants maintained sufficient mnemonic precision to distinguish perceptually similar items, validating our paradigm for examining precision decay.

To examine temporal effects on recognition accuracy, we analyzed performance across lag conditions using linear mixed-effects models (lme4: Bates, Mächler, Bolker, & Walker, 2015; lmerTest: Kuznetsova, Brockhoff, & Christensen, 2017). For repeated targets (Figure 2a), we included lag and similarity bin as fixed effects with participant random intercepts. Likelihood ratio tests confirmed significant effects of lag (χ²(2) = 71.86, p < .001), with performance declining as lag increased (β = -0.001, SE = 0.0004). Similarly, confusability bin significantly affected performance (χ²(2) = 1143, p < .001), with higher confusability bins showing reduced accuracy (β = -0.005, SE = 0.0001). These findings demonstrate that both temporal delay and perceptual similarity influence recognition memory, even for identical repetitions.

[Figure 2. Recognition performance as a function of lag and target-lure similarity. (a) Hit rates for repeated targets decline gradually with increasing lag. (b) Correct rejection rates for similar lures show a pronounced quadratic pattern, with sharp declines from lag 0 to lag 1 followed by gradual recovery. Data are stratified by model-predicted confusability tertiles: Bin 1 (low confusability), Bin 2 (medium confusability), and Bin 3 (high confusability). Error bars represent ±1 SEM.]

Our central hypothesis concerned the temporal dynamics of lure discrimination. Visual inspection of lure trial performance (Figure 2b) revealed the predicted curvilinear pattern: an initial sharp decline followed by gradual recovery. To test this formally, we compared mixed-effects models with linear versus quadratic lag terms. The quadratic model provided significantly better fit (χ²(5) = 28.78, p < .001), confirming that lure discrimination follows a non-monotonic trajectory over time. This quadratic pattern—characterized by rapid precision loss at minimal delays followed by gradual improvement—aligns with theoretical predictions about the differential decay rates of recollection and familiarity signals.

Crucially, we tested whether target-lure similarity modulated the temporal pattern of precision decay. Including the lag × confusability interaction significantly improved model fit (χ²(1) = 8.33, p = .004), confirming that similarity amplifies the temporal dynamics. Follow-up contrasts using emmeans (Searle, Speed, & Milliken, 1980) quantified this interaction by examining the lag 0 to lag 1 decline across confusability bins. The performance drop was significantly steeper for high-confusability lures (Δ = .095, SE = .031, 95% CI [.034, .155], t(6179) = 3.08, p = .002) and medium-confusability lures (Δ = .081, SE = .031, 95% CI [.021, .142], t(6179) = 2.64, p = .008) compared to low-confusability lures. High and medium confusability bins did not differ significantly (Δ = .014, SE = .031, 95% CI [-.047, .074], t(6181) = 0.44, p = .662). These results demonstrate that perceptual similarity specifically amplifies the rapid initial decay of mnemonic precision, supporting our hypothesis of a synergistic interaction between similarity-driven familiarity and temporal precision loss.

## 4. Discussion

This study provides the first systematic investigation of mnemonic precision decay at very short intervals using naturalistic visual stimuli. By adapting the continuous MST to include minimal lags, we revealed a striking temporal signature of precision loss that emerges within seconds of encoding. Our findings support three key conclusions. First, false alarm rates increased monotonically with target-lure similarity, validating our neural network-based similarity metric and replicating established MST findings. Second, lure discrimination followed a distinctive quadratic trajectory: exceptional precision at lag 0, catastrophic decline by lag 1, followed by gradual recovery at longer intervals. Third, and most critically, target-lure similarity amplified this temporal pattern—highly similar lures produced steeper initial declines in discrimination accuracy. This interaction between similarity and time reveals how rapidly precise episodic details become inaccessible, leaving recognition increasingly dependent on familiarity signals that similar lures readily trigger.

Our results contribute to understanding the computational mechanisms underlying pattern separation and pattern completion in human memory (Stark et al., 2019). Pattern separation—the ability to encode distinct representations from overlapping inputs—enables successful lure rejection by maintaining high-precision mnemonic traces. Conversely, pattern completion manifests as false recognition when partial cues trigger retrieval of similar stored representations (Yassa et al., 2011). Our finding that false alarm rates scale with target-lure similarity aligns with this framework: as perceptual overlap increases, shared features act as increasingly potent retrieval cues (Hunsaker & Kesner, 2013). This creates a computational tension—highly similar lures activate strong pattern completion signals that must be overcome through pattern separation. Our temporal findings suggest this balance shifts dramatically within seconds: at lag 0, pattern separation dominates, enabling precise discrimination; by lag 1, the loss of detailed representations tips the balance toward pattern completion, particularly for similar lures where overlapping features provide the strongest retrieval cues.

This study addresses a critical gap in the memory literature by systematically examining precision decay at minimal intervals. Previous continuous MST studies have focused on extended lags: Stark et al. (2015) tested intervals from 0-180 items to examine age-related interference effects, while Molitor and colleagues used 4, 12, and 40-item lags to characterize forgetting in clinical populations (Ally et al., 2013; Molitor et al., 2014). These studies consistently report linear declines in both target recognition and lure rejection, suggesting progressive shifts toward pattern completion. However, by excluding short intervals, they missed the dramatic non-monotonic dynamics we observed. The conventional assumption—that working memory maintenance ensures high precision at brief delays—proves only partially correct. While lag 0 performance confirms exceptional precision when items remain in active maintenance, the precipitous decline by lag 1 reveals that this precision advantage is remarkably fragile. This finding challenges simple capacity-based accounts of working memory and suggests that precision loss begins almost immediately as attention shifts to new inputs.

The quadratic pattern we observed parallels findings from verbal memory studies using conjunction lures (Jones & Atchley, 2002, 2006; Matzen et al., 2011), suggesting common principles govern precision decay across stimulus domains. Dual-process theory provides a compelling framework for understanding these dynamics (Jacoby, 1991; Yonelinas, 1994, 2002). According to this account, recognition operates through two dissociable processes with distinct temporal profiles: recollection of specific episodic details decays rapidly, while familiarity based on global similarity persists longer. At lag 0, participants can employ "recollect-to-reject" strategies, accessing detailed episodic traces to differentiate lures from targets. This precise comparison mechanism fails catastrophically by lag 1 as episodic details become inaccessible, leaving participants vulnerable to familiarity-based false recognition. The subsequent recovery at longer lags reflects weakening familiarity signals as both target and lure representations fade. Our similarity manipulation provides crucial evidence for this dual-process interpretation: highly similar lures evoke stronger familiarity signals that become particularly problematic when recollection fails, producing the steeper lag 0-1 decline we observed for high-similarity pairs.

The relationship between dual-process mechanisms and pattern separation remains contentious in the literature. While Kim and Yassa (2013) found that correct lure rejections elicited more "remember" than "know" responses in a remember/know MST variant, suggesting recollection supports pattern separation, subsequent findings have been inconsistent. Some studies implicate familiarity-based processes (Szőllősi, Bencze, & Racsmány, 2020), while others support recollection's primacy (Molitor et al., 2014; Schaeffer & Chek, 2022). Our temporal findings offer a potential resolution to this debate: the relative contributions of recollection and familiarity to pattern separation may depend critically on timing. At lag 0, successful discrimination likely relies on recollection-based comparisons between detailed representations. However, as these details rapidly decay, the system must increasingly rely on whatever signals remain available—including familiarity-based pattern separation mechanisms that may operate on coarser representations. This dynamic interplay suggests that pattern separation is not exclusively tied to either process but rather reflects the optimal use of available mnemonic information at each time point.

Our findings align remarkably well with Oberauer's concentric model of working memory (Oberauer, 2002, 2006, 2009), which proposes three hierarchical representational states: the focus of attention (single item, highest precision), the region of direct access (3-4 items, moderate precision), and activated long-term memory (unlimited capacity, lowest precision). The exceptional discrimination performance at lag 0 reflects the privileged status of the focus of attention. When a lure immediately follows its target, the target remains in the focus during the transition between trials, enabling high-fidelity comparisons. This transitional overlap—where the previous item briefly persists in focus while the new item is encoded—provides a unique opportunity for precise discrimination.

The catastrophic precision loss by lag 1 marks the transition from focused to peripheral representation. Once displaced from focus, the target moves to the region of direct access or activated long-term memory, where representations lack the resolution necessary for fine-grained discrimination. This architectural constraint explains why even a single intervening item fundamentally alters recognition dynamics. Moreover, the interaction with similarity suggests that transitions between representational states differentially affect various levels of detail: while coarse features supporting familiarity may survive the transition, the fine-grained details required to reject similar lures are selectively lost. This hierarchical degradation of precision as items move through working memory states provides a mechanistic account of our behavioral findings.

In conclusion, our study reveals that mnemonic precision follows a non-monotonic trajectory characterized by catastrophic early decline and gradual recovery. The sharp performance drop between lag 0 and lag 1 demonstrates that precision loss begins almost immediately as attention shifts to new inputs, challenging assumptions about working memory's protective effects. Crucially, this temporal dynamic is amplified by perceptual similarity, with highly similar lures producing steeper initial declines—evidence for a synergistic interaction between similarity-driven familiarity and time-dependent precision loss.

Several important questions remain. Our design cannot disentangle whether precision loss reflects temporal decay, interference from intervening items, or both (Hardt et al., 2013; Sadeh, Ozubko, Winocur, & Moscovitch, 2014). Future studies could vary presentation timing while holding item number constant to isolate these mechanisms. Additionally, investigating individual differences in precision maintenance could reveal whether the lag 0-1 transition represents a fundamental constraint or a flexible boundary. Finally, neuroimaging approaches could test whether the behavioral transitions we observed correspond to shifts in hippocampal pattern separation versus cortical familiarity signals. These findings advance our understanding of how quickly fine-grained memory representations become inaccessible in continuous experience.

Additionally, investigating individual differences in precision maintenance could reveal whether the lag 0-1 transition represents a fundamental constraint or a flexible boundary. Finally, neuroimaging approaches could test whether the behavioral transitions we observed correspond to shifts in hippocampal pattern separation versus cortical familiarity signals. These findings advance our understanding of how quickly fine-grained memory representations become inaccessible in continuous experience.

Future studies could vary presentation timing while holding item number constant to isolate these mechanisms. Additionally, investigating individual differences in precision maintenance could reveal whether the lag 0-1 transition represents a fundamental constraint or a flexible boundary. Finally, neuroimaging approaches could test whether the behavioral transitions we observed correspond to shifts in hippocampal pattern separation versus cortical familiarity signals. These findings advance our understanding of how quickly fine-grained memory representations become inaccessible in continuous experience.

(b) Correct rejection rates for similar lures show a pronounced quadratic pattern, with sharp declines from lag 0 to lag 1 followed by gradual recovery. Data are stratified by model-predicted confusability tertiles: Bin 1 (low confusability), Bin 2 (medium confusability), and Bin 3 (high confusability). Error bars represent ±1 SEM.
